blocks:
Ayahs

ٱقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسَابُهُمْ وَهُمْ فِى غَفْلَةٍ مُّعْرِضُونَ (١)
Mankind’s reckoning has drawn near to them, yet they are disregardful in [their] obliviousness. (1)
مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِم مُّحْدَثٍ إِلَّا ٱسْتَمَعُوهُ وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (٢)
There does not come to them any new reminder from their Lord but they listen to it as they play around, (2)
لَاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّجْوَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ هَلْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ ۖ أَفَتَأْتُونَ ٱلسِّحْرَ وَأَنتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ (٣)
their hearts set on diversions. The wrongdoers secretly whisper together, [saying], ‘Is this [man] not a human being like yourselves? Will you give in to magic with open eyes?’ (3)
قَالَ رَبِّى يَعْلَمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٤)
He said, ‘My Lord knows every word [spoken] in the sky and on the earth, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing.’ (4)
بَلْ قَالُوٓا۟ أَضْغَـٰثُ أَحْلَـٰمٍۭ بَلِ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ بَلْ هُوَ شَاعِرٌ فَلْيَأْتِنَا بِـَٔايَةٍ كَمَآ أُرْسِلَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (٥)
But they said, ‘[They are] muddled dreams!’ ‘Indeed, he has fabricated it!’ ‘Indeed, he is a poet!’ ‘Let him bring us a sign, like those sent to the former generations.’ (5)
مَآ ءَامَنَتْ قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَآ ۖ أَفَهُمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦)
No town that We destroyed before them believed. Will these then have faith [if they are sent signs]? (6)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٧)
We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know. (7)
وَمَا جَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ جَسَدًا لَّا يَأْكُلُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ خَـٰلِدِينَ (٨)
We did not make them bodies that did not eat food, nor were they immortal. (8)
ثُمَّ صَدَقْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْوَعْدَ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَمَن نَّشَآءُ وَأَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٩)
Then We fulfilled Our promise to them, and We delivered them and whomever We wished, and We destroyed the transgressors. (9)
لَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ كِتَـٰبًا فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٠)
Certainly We have sent down to you a Book in which there is an admonition for you. Do you not exercise your reason? (10)
وَكَمْ قَصَمْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ كَانَتْ ظَالِمَةً وَأَنشَأْنَا بَعْدَهَا قَوْمًا ءَاخَرِينَ (١١)
How many a town We have smashed that had been wrongdoing, and We brought forth another people after it. (11)
فَلَمَّآ أَحَسُّوا۟ بَأْسَنَآ إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَرْكُضُونَ (١٢)
So when they sighted Our punishment, behold, they ran away from it. (12)
لَا تَرْكُضُوا۟ وَٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أُتْرِفْتُمْ فِيهِ وَمَسَـٰكِنِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْـَٔلُونَ (١٣)
‘Do not run away! Return to the opulence you were given to enjoy and to your dwellings so that you may be questioned!’ (13)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (١٤)
They said, ‘Woe to us! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (14)
فَمَا زَالَت تِّلْكَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ حَصِيدًا خَـٰمِدِينَ (١٥)
That remained their cry until We turned them into a mown field, stilled [like burnt ashes]. (15)

(Commentary)

The purpose of this Surah is to discuss prophethood (النبوة), grounding this discourse in monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final judgment of all souls. It opens by mentioning the approach of the reckoning and the people's heedlessness of it, as well as their turning away from the true call that contains the heavenly revelation (الوحي السماوي); for this revelation is the criterion for the reckoning on the Day of Reckoning. From there, it transitions to the subject of prophethood and the people's mockery of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prophethood, their charging him with being merely a human sorcerer, or that what he brought was but a jumble of dreams, or that he is a fabricator, or even a poet! The Surah refutes this by mentioning the general, universal characteristics of past prophets, showing that the Prophet lacks nothing of what they possessed, nor does what he brought differ from what they brought.

Then, it mentions the stories of a group of prophets to support the aforementioned generalities: Moses, Aaron, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Lot, Noah, David, Solomon, Job, Ishmael, Idris, Dhul-Kifl, Dhul-Nun, Zechariah, John, and Jesus.

Subsequently, it concludes by mentioning the Day of Reckoning and what the guilty and the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who possess godwariness (تقوى), the innate disposition toward truth and protection against sin — will encounter therein. It affirms that the ultimate outcome is for the God-conscious and that the earth shall be inherited by His righteous servants. It then mentions that their turning away from prophethood is only due to their turning away from monotheism, establishing proof for the latter just as it establishes it for the former. The dominant tone in the Surah is that of threat (الوعيد) over promise (الوعد), and warning (الإنذار) over glad tidings (التبشير). The Surah is Meccan without any disagreement, and the context of its verses bears witness to this.

His saying, the Exalted: اقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسابُهُمْ وَهُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مُعْرِضُونَ — "The reckoning of mankind has drawn near to them, yet they are oblivious and turning away." (21:1). The word "approaching" (الاقتراب) is a verbal form (افتعال) derived from "closeness" (القرب). Both iqtaraba and qaruba carry the same meaning, except that iqtaraba is more emphatic due to its increased morphological structure, indicating a heightened concern with the closeness. Both terms can be transitive using the prepositions "from" (من) and "to" (إلى). It is said: "Zayd drew near from (من) or to (إلى) 'Amr." The first indicates taking the ratio of closeness from 'Amr's side, while the second indicates taking it from Zayd's side, because the primary meaning of min is the starting point of an end, just as the primary meaning of ila is its termination.

From this, it becomes clear that the lam in "to mankind" (لِلنَّاسِ) carries the meaning of "to" (إلى) rather than "from" (من), because what is appropriate for the context is taking the ratio of approach from the side of the reckoning; for it is the reckoning that seeks the people by drawing near to them, while the people are in heedlessness, turning away.

What is meant by "the reckoning" (الحساب) — which is Allah's (سبحانه) auditing of their deeds on the Day of Resurrection — is the reckoning itself, not its time by way of metaphor or by assuming the word "time" is omitted, even if some commentators insist upon this. Others justify it by saying that time is the primary basis for closeness and distance, and these qualities are only attributed to the events occurring within it through its mediation.

This is because the purpose here is concerned with the remembrance of the reckoning itself due to its connection to the deeds of the people, as they are responsible for their actions. Thus, it was necessary in Wisdom that a reminder (ذكر) should be sent down to them from their Lord to alert them to that for which they are responsible. It was likewise mandatory upon them to listen to it earnestly, not playfully or with distracted hearts. Indeed, had the speech been intended to describe the terrors of the Hour and the punishment prepared for the guilty, it would have been more appropriate to use the expression "the Day of Reckoning" or to imply the word "time" and the like.

The word "mankind" (الناس) refers to the genus, meaning the human society, most of whom were polytheists at that time, rather than the polytheists specifically. Even if the described attributes — such as heedlessness (الغفلة), turning away (الإعراض), and mockery — are attributes of the polytheists, this is not a case of metaphorically attributing the judgment of a part to the whole. Rather, it is the literal attribution of a judgment to the society itself, followed by the exception of the part that does not possess that attribute. This is indicated by verses such as: وَأَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا — "And the wrongdoers confer in secret" (21:3) and His saying: فَأَنْجَيْناهُمْ وَمَنْ نَشاءُ — "Then We delivered them and whomever We wished" (21:9), following the Quranic method in its social addresses of attributing a judgment to the society and then exempting the individuals not characterized by it.

In short, there is a difference between taking the society as the subject of a judgment while exempting individuals not characterized by it, and taking the majority of individuals as the subject and then metaphorically attributing the judgment to the whole. Our current case is of the first type, not the second.

Some have explained the approach of the reckoning for mankind by saying that every day that passes for the world makes it closer to the reckoning than it was yesterday. It has also been said that the approach is in consideration of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) mission being in the final era, as he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I and the Hour have been sent like these two" [joining his fingers]. As for the previous explanation, it only suits a word indicating continuity, rather than the past tense which indicates the completion of its realization. Similar to this is the explanation that the approach is due to the certainty of its occurrence, for everything that is coming is near.

His saying: وَهُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مُعْرِضُونَ — "yet they are oblivious and turning away" (21:1) means they have attached themselves to the world and occupied themselves with its enjoyments. Their hearts became so filled with its love that no space remained for the remembrance of the reckoning to settle in a way that would affect them. Thus, even if they were reminded, they would not remember, and this is heedlessness (الغفلة). For a thing may be neglected because it is not conceptualized at all, or it may be neglected because it is not conceptualized as it truly deserves to be, such that the soul is affected by it.

Through this, the answer to the objection becomes clear: that combining heedlessness (which necessitates a lack of awareness) and turning away (which necessitates awareness) is a combination of two contradictory states. The summary of the answer is that they are in heedlessness of the reckoning because they do not conceptualize it as it truly is, and they are turning away from it because they are occupied with things that contradict the requirements of knowledge.

Al-Zamakhshari answered this by saying: "He described them with heedlessness along with turning away in the sense that they are heedless of their reckoning, distracted, not reflecting on their end, nor perceiving where the conclusion of their affair leads, despite their intellects necessitating that there must be a reward for the doer of good and the doer of evil. When the staff is struck for them and they are alerted from the slumber of heedlessness and made to perceive this through the verses and warnings recited to them, they turn away, block their ears, and flee." End quote.

The difference between his explanation and ours is that he took the turning away to be subsequent to the heedlessness, whereas the apparent meaning of the verse is that both coexist for them at the same time, not that the two attributes are chronologically ordered.

Others resolved the problem by taking "turning away" (إعراض) to mean "vastness" (اتساع), meaning they are vast in their heedlessness. Others took "heedlessness" (غفلة) to mean "neglect" (إهمال), and there is no contradiction between neglect and turning away. Both these views are instances of committing to what is unnecessary.

The meaning is: the reckoning of their deeds has drawn near to mankind, while they are in a state of continuous or immense heedlessness, turning away from it by occupying themselves with the distractions of the world and failing to prepare for it through repentance, faith, and godwariness (تقوى).

His saying, the Exalted: ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ ذِكْرٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا اسْتَمَعُوهُ وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ لاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ — "There does not come to them any new reminder from their Lord but they listen to it while they play, with their hearts diverted." (21:2-3). This verse serves as a justification for His saying: "yet they are oblivious and turning away." For if they were not in heedlessness and turning away, they would not play or be distracted when listening to the reminder (الذكر) — the divine message that alerts them to truth — which only alerts them to what is vital for them to notice and for which they must prepare. Therefore, the sentence is presented as a separate clause without a conjunction.

What is meant by "the reminder" (الذكر) is that by which Allah (سبحانه) reminds, namely divine revelation such as the heavenly books, including the Noble Quran. Its "coming to them" refers to its descent upon the Prophet and its being heard and conveyed. "New" (محدث) means recent, which is a relative meaning and an attribute of the reminder. The Quran, for example, is a new reminder that came to them after the Gospel (الإنجيل), and the Gospel was a new reminder that came after the Torah (التوراة). Likewise, some Surahs and verses of the Quran are a new reminder that came after others.

His saying: إِلَّا اسْتَمَعُوهُ — "but they listen to it" is an exception (استثناء مفرغ) from all their states. "They listen to it" is a circumstantial clause (حال), and وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ — "while they play" and لاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ — "with their hearts diverted" are two circumstantial clauses referring to the plural pronoun in "they listen to it"; thus, they are nested circumstantial clauses.

Play (اللعب) is an action with organized parts that has no goal other than imagination, like the play of children. Diversion (اللهو) is your preoccupation with something that distracts you from what is important. It is said: "Such a thing diverted him," meaning it occupied him away from his vital concerns. For this reason, musical instruments are called "instruments of diversion" (آلات اللهو) or "places of diversion" (ملاهي). Diversion is an attribute of the heart, which is why He said: "with their hearts diverted," attributing it to their hearts.

The meaning of the verse is: No new reminder comes to them — through descent and reaching them — in any state whatsoever, except that they are in a state of playing with diverted hearts, listening to it in that condition. That is, the origination and renewal of the reminder does not affect them even slightly, nor does it prevent them from being occupied with the play of the world instead of what lies beyond it. This is an allusion to the fact that the reminder does not affect them in any state, not that its newness does not affect them while its oldness does; this is self-evident.

The literal meaning of the verse has been used as evidence for the Quran being "originated" (محدث) and not "eternal" (قديم). The Ash'arites interpreted it by saying that describing the reminder as "new" or "originated" is in respect to its descent, which does not contradict its being eternal in itself. The literal meaning of the verse stands against them, and we shall provide a completion of this discussion in an independent inquiry.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of the Origination and Eternity of Speech in Several Sections)

1 — What is the meaning of the origination (حدوث) and persistence (بقاء) of speech? If we hear speech from a speaker, such as a poem from a poet, we immediately attribute it to him. Then, if he repeats it and speaks the same again, we do not doubt that it is his very first speech which he has repeated. Then, if a narrator conveys it from him, we judge that it is the very speech of that first speaker. Thus, however much the narration is repeated, the conveyed speech is identically the first speech issued by the first speaker, even if repeated infinitely.

This is based on what conventional understanding (الفهم العرفي) dictates. However, if we reflect a little deeper, we find the reality of the matter to be otherwise. A speaker's saying "Zayd came to me," for example, is not a single speech, because it contains the letters jim, alif, or hamza. Each of these is an individual instance of sound formed by the speaker's breath relying on one of the points of articulation in his mouth. The whole consists of many sounds that are not one at all, except by way of convention (الوضع) and consideration (الاعتبار).

Furthermore, that which the first speaker said a second time, and that which the narrator conveys a third or fourth time, are other individual instances of sound similar to the sounds formed in the first assumed speech; they are not identical to them except by way of consideration and a type of figurative extension.

These sounds are not "speech" except insofar as they are signs (علائم) and indicators (أمارات) according to convention and consideration, pointing to mental meanings. They are not "one" except by virtue of a single purpose being attached to them.

It follows that speech, insofar as it is speech, is a conventional, considerational matter (أمر وضعي اعتباري) that has no realization in the external world outside the realm of claim and consideration. What is actually realized in the external world are the individual instances of sound that have been made into signs by convention and consideration, insofar as they are sounds, not insofar as they are established signs. Realization is only attributed to speech through a kind of figurative regard.

From here, it becomes clear that speech is not characterized by either origination (الحدوث) or persistence (البقاء). For origination — which is the precedence of non-existence over existence in time — and persistence — which is the continued existence of a thing moment after moment in a continuous manner — are among the properties of external realities (الحقائق الخارجية), and considerational matters have no realization in the external world.

Likewise, speech is not characterized by eternity (القدم) — which is the existence of a thing not preceded by temporal non-existence — because eternity, like origination, is a property of external realities, not considerational matters.

Moreover, characterizing speech as eternal faces another specific problem: speech is composed of letters arranged in a sequence, some before and some after. Precedence and subsequence are inconceivable in eternity; otherwise, the subsequent would be originated (حادث) while being eternal (قديم), which is a contradiction. Thus, speech — in the sense of composed letters indicating a complete meaning by convention — cannot be conceived as eternal, as this is inherently impossible. Understand this well.

2 — Is speech, insofar as it is speech, an act (فعل) or an essential attribute (صفة ذاتية)? Meaning, is the essence of the speaker complete in itself, independent of speech, such that speech branches out from it? Or is the subsistence of the essence dependent upon it, like the dependence of an animal's essence on life, or like the inseparability of the number four from evenness? There is no doubt that speech, in reality, is neither an act nor an attribute of the speaker, because it is a considerational matter that has no realization except in the realm of claim and convention. Thus, it cannot be a real act issued from an external essence, nor an attribute of an external subject.

Yes, speech, insofar as it is a title for an external matter — namely the composed sounds, which are external acts of the one producing them — is considered an act of the speaker by way of extension. Then, from its relation to the agent, an attribute is derived for him, which is "speaking" (التكلم) and "addressing" (التكليم), as is the case with its counterparts among considerational matters like humility, honoring, insulting, buying, and selling.

3 — Analysis of the meaning of speech: It is possible to analyze speech from the perspective of its purpose, which is to disclose the meanings hidden in the mind. In this way, it returns to being a real matter after having been considerational. This is a process applicable to most or all considerational matters, and the Quran has used it for many concepts such as prostration (السجود), obedience (القنوت), willingness and compulsion, sovereignty (الملك), the Throne (العرش), the Pedestal (الكرسي), the Book (الكتاب), and others.

Thus, the reality of speech is that by which the hidden contents of the mind are disclosed. Every effect (معلول) is "speech" for its cause (علة), because by its existence it discloses the cause's perfection hidden in its essence. More subtly, the essential attributes of a thing are its "speech," disclosing the hidden nature of its essence. This is what philosophers mention: that His (the Exalted) essential attributes, such as knowledge, power, and life, are His speech, and likewise, the world is His speech.

It is clear that speech, based on this analysis, follows the nature of its existence in its eternity or origination. Divine knowledge is eternal speech by the eternity of the Essence. The originated Zayd, insofar as he is a sign (آية) disclosing his Lord, is His originated speech. The revelation (الوحي) descending upon the Prophet, insofar as it is a divine making-to-understand (تفهيم), is originated by the origination of that understanding. Insofar as it is in the knowledge of Allah — and His knowledge is considered His speech — it is eternal by the eternity of the Essence, like His knowledge of all things, originated and eternal.

4 — It follows from the preceding sections that if the Noble Quran is intended to mean these verses we recite, insofar as they are speech indicating mental meanings like all other speech, it is in reality neither originated nor eternal. Yes, it is characterized by origination due to the origination of the sounds that are titled with the name of speech and Quran.

If it is intended to mean the true meanings within the knowledge of Allah, it is like His knowledge of every true thing: eternal by His eternity. Thus, the Quran is eternal — meaning His knowledge of it is eternal — just as the originated Zayd is eternal — meaning His knowledge of him is eternal.

From here, it becomes clear that the debate over the eternity or origination of the Quran, insofar as it is the speech of Allah, is of no benefit. For the one who asserts eternity, if he means that the recited verses, insofar as they are composed sounds indicating their meanings, are eternal and not preceded by non-existence, then he is being obstinate. If he means that it is in the knowledge of Allah — in other words, that His knowledge of His Book is eternal — then there is no reason to append His knowledge to it and then judge it as eternal; rather, His knowledge of everything is eternal by the eternity of His Essence, because what is meant by this knowledge is the Essential Knowledge (العلم الذاتي).

Furthermore, there is no reason then to count speech as another positive essential attribute (صفة ثبوتية ذاتية) of His (the Exalted) besides knowledge, since it reduces to it. If it were permissible for us to count everything that applies through analysis to some of His real positive attributes as a positive attribute, the number of positive attributes would be limitless. Such analysis would be possible for attributes like manifestation, hiddenness, greatness, splendor, light, beauty, perfection, completeness, and simplicity, among countless others.

That which the Law (الشرع) has considered and which has appeared in this term in the Noble Quran is evident in the first mentioned meaning, without analysis, such as His saying: تِلْكَ الرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ كَلَّمَ اللهُ — "These are the apostles, some of whom We gave an advantage over others: of them are those to whom Allah spoke" (2:253), and His saying: وَكَلَّمَ اللهُ مُوسى تَكْلِيماً — "and Allah spoke to Moses with a [direct] speech" (4:164), and His saying: وَقَدْ كانَ فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ كَلامَ اللهِ ثُمَّ يُحَرِّفُونَهُ — "while a group of them would hear the word of Allah and then, after understanding it, they would pervert it" (2:75), and His saying: يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَواضِعِهِ — "they pervert the words from their contexts" (5:13).

As for what some have mentioned — that there is a "psychic speech" (كلام نفسي) subsisting in the soul of the speaker other than the verbal speech, quoting the poet:

Indeed, speech is in the heart, and the tongue Was only made as a guide to the heart

—and that it is this psychic speech in Him (the Exalted) that is described as eternal, not the verbal speech:

The response is that if by "psychic speech" they mean the meaning of the verbal speech or its mental image that corresponds to its wording, then its meaning returns to knowledge and it is not something additional to it or a distinct attribute. If they mean a meaning beyond that, we do not recognize it in our own souls when we examine them.

As for the poetry quoted in a rational discussion, it neither benefits them nor harms us; rational inquiries are of a higher standing than to be contested by poets.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا هَلْ هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ أَفَتَأْتُونَ السِّحْرَ وَأَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ — "And the wrongdoers confer in secret: ‘Is this [man] anything but a human being like yourselves? Will you then yield to magic while you see [it]?’" (21:3). "Keeping secret" (الإسرار) is the opposite of "announcing." Thus, "keeping the secret talk secret" (إسرار النجوى) is an emphasis on concealing and hiding the speech. Since "keeping the speech secret" already implies the meaning of secret talk (النجوى), adding it to najwa provides emphasis.

The subject pronoun in "they kept the secret talk secret" (أَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى) refers to the people. However, since the action was not performed by all of them, nor even by most — for among them were the weak and those not concerned with it — even though it is attributed to the whole by virtue of the heedlessness and turning away present in their society, He clarified the attribution by saying: الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا — "the wrongdoers". This is an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) by which He indicated that the secret talk was specifically among those who did wrong.

His saying: هَلْ هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ أَفَتَأْتُونَ السِّحْرَ وَأَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ — "‘Is this [man] anything but a human being like yourselves? Will you then yield to magic while you see [it]?’" is what they whispered in their secret talk. They used to openly declare their denial of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and announce that he was a human and that the Quran was magic, without hiding any of that. However, they kept it secret in their najwa because it was a consultation (شورى) among them, where they consulted one another: how should they confront the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and answer his call to faith in Allah and his message? They had no choice but to conceal what was mentioned among them, even if they announced it after agreeing to reject the call.

Their secret talk included two statements they concluded upon or presented as rhetorical questions. The first is: هَلْ هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ — "Is this [man] anything but a human being like yourselves?" They took this as an argument to invalidate his prophethood: that as you observe him — and they used the demonstrative pronoun "this" (هذا) rather than "he" to indicate knowledge of him through observation — he is a human like you, not differing from you in anything specific to him. If his claim of connection to the Unseen (الغيب) and link to the Divine were true, you would have the same, for you are humans like him. Since you have no news of that, he is like you and has no news; thus, he is not a prophet as he claims.

Their second statement: أَفَتَأْتُونَ السِّحْرَ وَأَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ — "Will you then yield to magic while you see [it]?" is branched with the "f" of consequence (فاء التفريع) upon the denial of prophethood by affirming his humanity. The meaning returns to this: since he is not a prophet connected to the Unseen, that which he brought you claiming it to be a sign of prophethood is not a miraculous sign from Allah, but rather magic that you are unable to replicate. It is not fitting for one with sound vision to submit to magic and believe in a magician.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّي يَعْلَمُ الْقَوْلَ فِي السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — "He said, ‘My Lord knows [every] word in the heaven and the earth, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing.’" (21:4). That is, He (the Exalted) encompasses in knowledge every word, whether secret or open, and in any place. He is the All-hearing of your words, the All-knowing of your deeds; thus, the matter belongs to Him, and I have no part in the matter.

The verse is a narration of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) words to them when they conferred in secret and decided upon denying his prophethood and charging his sign — which is his Book — with magic. In it, the matter is referred back to Allah (سبحانه), as is the case in most instances where they proposed a sign to him. This was also the way of other prophets, as in His saying: قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَإِنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ — "Say, ‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I am only a manifest warner.’" (67:26), and His saying: قالَ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ — "He said, ‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I communicate to you what I have been sent with.’" (46:23), and His saying: قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْآياتُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَإِنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ — "Say, ‘The signs are only with Allah, and I am only a manifest warner.’" (29:50).

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ قالُوا أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ بَلِ افْتَراهُ بَلْ هُوَ شاعِرٌ فَلْيَأْتِنا بِآيَةٍ كَما أُرْسِلَ الْأَوَّلُونَ — "‘No,’ they said, ‘[they are] a jumble of dreams!’ ‘No, he has fabricated it!’ ‘No, he is a poet!’ ‘Let him bring us a sign such as the former ones were sent [with].’" (21:5). This shows their progression in accusation and denial. Their saying "a jumble of dreams" (أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ) means a mixture of disorganized visions he saw and merely thought were prophethood and a book; thus, his case is lighter than magic. Their saying بَلِ افْتَراهُ — "No, he has fabricated it!" is an escalation from the previous, for "jumble of dreams" implies the matter was confused and ambiguous to him, but "fabrication" necessitates intentionality. Their saying بَلْ هُوَ شاعِرٌ — "No, he is a poet!" is an escalation from another angle; for the fabricator speaks after reflection and deliberation, but the poet utters what his imagination suggests and aims for what his feelings beautify for him without reflection. He might praise the ugly for its ugliness, or disparage the beautiful for its beauty, or deny the necessary, or insist upon pure falsehood, or validate a lie or belie the truth.

Their saying: فَلْيَأْتِنا بِآيَةٍ كَما أُرْسِلَ الْأَوَّلُونَ — "Let him bring us a sign such as the former ones were sent [with]" is branched upon what preceded it. What is meant by "the former ones" are the past prophets. That is: if this which he brought and considers a sign — namely the Quran — is but a jumble of dreams, a fabrication, or poetry, then his claim to prophethood is not established by it, nor does it convince us. So let him bring us a sign as the former ones brought, such as the She-camel, the Staff, and the White Hand.

In His saying: "such as the former ones were sent [with]," where the context might have suggested "as the former ones brought," there is an indication that the sign is an inseparable requirement of being sent. So if he were a messenger, let him follow the example of the former ones in the proofs they used for their messages.

The polytheists among the idolaters denied prophethood from the outset. Thus, the statement of these people: "Let him bring us a sign such as the former ones were sent [with]" is a clear proof that they were bewildered in their affair, not knowing what to do. Sometimes they confront him with mockery, sometimes they are arbitrary, and sometimes they say what contradicts their own beliefs, proposing a sign from the signs of the former ones while they do not believe in their messages nor acknowledge their signs. In their saying: "Let him bring us a sign such as the former ones were sent [with]," there is also an implicit promise of faith if he were to bring one of the proposed signs.

His saying, the Exalted: ما آمَنَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها أَفَهُمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Not a town that We destroyed before them believed. Will they then believe?" (21:6). This is a refutation and denial of the implicit promise of faith contained in their saying: "Let him bring us a sign such as the former ones were sent [with]."

The summary of the meaning as provided by the context is that they are lying in their promise. If We were to send down any of the signs of the former ones they proposed, they would not believe in them, and it would result in their destruction. For the former people of the towns proposed them, and We sent them down, but they did not believe in them, so We destroyed them. The nature of these people is the nature of their predecessors in excess (الإسراف) and arrogance (الاستكبار); thus, they are not going to believe. The verse is similar in one respect to His saying: فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "But they would not believe in what they had denied before." (10:74).

Accordingly, there is an omission and brevity in the verse. The implied meaning is something like: "No people of a town before them believed who proposed signs which We then sent down to them and destroyed them when they did not believe after the descent. Will they — meaning the Arab polytheists — then believe, when they are like them in excess?" Describing the town with His saying: أَهْلَكْناها — "We destroyed" is a description by the final state they reached, to indicate that the consequence of answering what they proposed is destruction and nothing else.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ — "We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know." (21:7). This is a response to their argument for denying his prophethood (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by their saying: "Is this [man] anything but a human being like yourselves?" The response is that the past prophets were nothing but men from among humans; thus, humanity does not contradict prophethood.

Describing them as "men" (رِجالاً) with His saying: نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ — "to whom We revealed" is to point out the difference between prophets and others. Its summary is that the only difference between a prophet and another is that We reveal to the prophets and not to others. Revelation (الوحي) is a special gift and favor; it need not encompass every human such that if it is realized, it must be realized in all, and if it is not found in one, it is not found in any. Thus, you cannot judge its absence in yourselves as proof of its absence in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is like all other specific attributes found only in certain individuals among humans, which cannot be denied.

The verse resolves into two arguments that invalidate their deduction from his humanity to the denial of his prophethood:

One is the refutation of their argument by pointing to men from among humans who were prophets; thus, there is no contradiction between humanity and prophethood.

The second is by way of resolution: that the distinction between a prophet and another is not an attribute that does not exist in humans, or that if it exists, it must exist in all. Rather, it is divine revelation, which is a special honor and favor from Allah that He grants to whomever He wills. In this view, the verse is similar to His saying: قالُوا إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا — "They said, ‘You are nothing but humans like us’" — until He said — قالَتْ لَهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ إِنْ نَحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَمُنُّ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ — "Their apostles said to them, ‘We are indeed nothing but humans like you, but Allah favors whomever He wishes...’" (14:10-11).

His saying: فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ — "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know" is a support and reinforcement for His saying: "We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men." That is: if you already know this, then so be it; but if you do not know, then refer to the People of the Reminder and ask them: were the former prophets anything but men from among humans?

What is meant by "the Reminder" (الذكر) is the heavenly Book, and by "the People of the Reminder" (أهل الذكر) are the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). For they used to side with the polytheists in enmity toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the polytheists used to hold them in high regard, sometimes consulting them about him and asking them for questions to test him. They are the ones who said to the polytheists regarding the Muslims: هؤُلاءِ أَهْدى مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَبِيلاً — "These are better guided on the way than the faithful." (4:51). The address in His saying "Ask..." etc., is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and everyone who hears this address, whether learned or ignorant, in order to support the statement, which is common in speech.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما جَعَلْناهُمْ جَسَداً لا يَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعامَ وَما كانُوا خالِدِينَ — "We did not make them bodies that did not eat food, nor were they immortal." (21:8). That is, they were men from among humans, and We did not strip them of human properties by making them a body (جسداً) devoid of the spirit of life that does not eat or drink, nor did We protect them from death such that they would be immortal. Rather, they are humans from among those He created, eating food — which is a necessary property — and dying, which is like eating.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ صَدَقْناهُمُ الْوَعْدَ فَأَنْجَيْناهُمْ وَمَنْ نَشاءُ وَأَهْلَكْنَا الْمُسْرِفِينَ — "Then We fulfilled the promise to them, and We delivered them and whomever We wished, and We destroyed the profligate." (21:9). This is conjoined to His previous saying: "We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men." It contains an explanation of the consequence of sending them and what the affair of the profligate (المسرفين) among their nations — who proposed signs to them — led to. It also clarifies what was alluded to regarding their destruction in His saying: "Not a town that We destroyed," and serves as a threat to the polytheists.

What is meant by "the promise" (الوعد) in His saying: "Then We fulfilled the promise to them" is what He promised them of victory for their religion and the exaltation of their word, the word of truth, as in His saying: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ وَإِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors." (37:171-173), among other verses.

His saying: فَأَنْجَيْناهُمْ وَمَنْ نَشاءُ — "and We delivered them and whomever We wished" refers to the messengers and the believers, to whom He promised salvation, as indicated by His saying: حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نُنْجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "it is a must for Us to deliver the faithful." (10:103). The "profligate" (المسرفون) are the polytheists who exceeded the bounds of servitude. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ كِتاباً فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ — "We have certainly sent down to you a Book in which is your reminder. Will you not then exercise your reason?" (21:10). This is a favor from Him (the Exalted) by sending down the Quran to this nation. What is meant by "their reminder" (ذِكْرُكُمْ) is the reminder specific to them, suited to their state, which is the ultimate extent of high true knowledge that the human capacity can hold, and the most upright Law of the Primordial Nature (الشريعة الحنيفية) that can be implemented in human society. The address is to the entire nation.

It has been said: what is meant by "reminder" (الذكر) is honor (الشرف). The meaning would be: in it is your honor if you hold fast to it, you will be remembered by it, as His saying was interpreted: وَإِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ — "It is indeed a reminder for you and for your people" (43:44). The address would then be to all the believers or to the Arabs specifically because the Quran was sent down in their language; however, this is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَمْ قَصَمْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ كانَتْ ظالِمَةً — "How many a town We have shattered that was wrongdoing..." (21:11) until the end of the five verses. Shattering (القصم) originally means breaking. It is said: "He shattered his back," meaning he broke it. It is used as an allusion for destruction. Bringing forth (الإنشاء) is origination. Perceiving (الإحساس) is perception through the senses. Might (البأس) is punishment. Sprinting (الركض) is running with heavy footsteps. Luxury (الإتراف) is expansion in blessings. Harvested (الحصيد) is that which is cut down, from which the harvesting of crops is derived. Extinguished (الخمود) is stillness and silence.

The meaning is: "How many We have shattered" and destroyed "of a town" — meaning its people — "that was wrongdoing" to itself through excess and disbelief. "And We brought forth" and originated "another people. And when they perceived" and found through their senses — meaning the people of the wrongdoing town — "Our might" and Our punishment, "behold, they were sprinting from it," running away like those defeated. It is said to them by way of rebuke and reprimand: "Do not sprint, but return to that in which you were given luxury" of blessings "and your dwellings" — and to your dwellings — "that you may be questioned." That is: perhaps the poor and those in need will rush to you with requests, and you may act arrogantly toward them, or act with vanity, or hide from them. This is an allusion to their pride and superiority, and the fact that the followed ones considered themselves lords over the followers besides Allah.

"They said" in regret: "Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers." "So that remained" — which is their cry "Woe to us!" containing the acknowledgment of His (the Exalted) Lordship and their own wrongdoing — "their cry until We made them harvested" — cut down — "extinguished" — still and silent, like fire when it is extinguished; no voice is heard from them, nor is any fame mentioned of them.

His saying: "that you may be questioned" has been explained in other ways far from understanding, which we have declined to mention.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ihtijaj, it is narrated from Safwan ibn Yahya that he said: Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) said to Abu Qurrah, the companion of Shubrumah: "The Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, the Criterion (الفرقان), and every book that was sent down — was the speech of Allah which He sent down to all the worlds as a light and guidance. They are all originated (محدثة) and they are other than Allah, as He says: أَوْ يُحْدِثُ لَهُمْ ذِكْراً — 'or it may produce for them a reminder' (20:113), and He said: ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ ذِكْرٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا اسْتَمَعُوهُ وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ — 'There does not come to them any new reminder from their Lord but they listen to it while they play' (21:2). Allah originated all the books He sent down."

Abu Qurrah asked: "Will they then perish?" Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) replied: "The Muslims have agreed that whatever is other than Allah is the act of Allah — and the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Criterion are the act of Allah. Have you not heard people saying: 'Lord of the Quran'? And that the Quran says on the Day of Resurrection: 'O Lord, this is so-and-so' — and it knows him better than he knows himself — 'I made his day thirsty and his night sleepless, so grant me intercession for him'? Likewise, the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms are all originated and nurtured (مربوبة) — He who has nothing like Him originated them for a people who exercise reason. Whoever claims they have always existed has declared that Allah is not the First, the Eternal, nor the One, and that speech has always existed with Him and has no beginning." (The hadith).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ — "with their hearts diverted," he said: "From diversion (التلهي)."

In the same source, regarding His saying: ما آمَنَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها ـ أَفَهُمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Not a town that We destroyed before them believed. Will they then believe?" he said: "How will they believe when those before them did not believe in the signs until they perished?"

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ — "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know": "Who is meant by that?" He said: "We are." I said: "So you are the ones to be asked?" He said: "Yes." I said: "And we are the questioners?" He said: "Yes." I said: "So it is upon us to ask you?" He said: "Yes." I said: "So it is upon you to answer us?" He said: "No — that is up to us; if we wish, we do so, and if we wish, we refrain." Then he said: هذا عَطاؤُنا فَامْنُنْ أَوْ أَمْسِكْ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ — "This is Our gift: so bestow or withhold without any reckoning." (38:39).

I say: Al-Tabarsi narrated this meaning in Majma' al-Bayan from 'Ali and Abu Ja'far (peace be upon them), saying: "It is supported by the fact that Allah (the Exalted) named the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) a 'reminder' (ذكراً) and a 'messenger'."

This is an instance of conceptual application (الجري), for it is necessary that the verse is not specific. "The Reminder" is either the Quran, or the heavenly books in general, or divine knowledge; and they [the Ahl al-Bayt] are its people in any case. This is not an interpretation of the verse according to the occasion of revelation (مورد النزول), for there would be no meaning in referring the polytheists to the people of the Messenger or the people of the Quran when they were their adversaries; had they accepted from them, they would have accepted from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) himself.

In Rawdat al-Kafi, in a discourse by 'Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) on exhortation and asceticism in the world, he says: "Allah has indeed made you hear in His Book what He did to the wrongdoing people of the towns before you, where He said: وَكَمْ قَصَمْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ كانَتْ ظالِمَةً — 'How many a town We have shattered that was wrongdoing.' He only meant the people of the town when He said: وَأَنْشَأْنا بَعْدَها قَوْماً آخَرِينَ — 'and We brought forth after it another people.' Then He (Mighty and Majestic) said: فَلَمَّا أَحَسُّوا بَأْسَنا إِذا هُمْ مِنْها يَرْكُضُونَ — 'And when they perceived Our might, behold, they were sprinting from it,' meaning they were fleeing. He said: لا تَرْكُضُوا وَارْجِعُوا إِلى ما أُتْرِفْتُمْ فِيهِ وَمَساكِنِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْئَلُونَ — 'Do not sprint, but return to that in which you were given luxury and your dwellings, that you may be questioned.' When the punishment came to them, قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ فَما زالَتْ تِلْكَ دَعْواهُمْ حَتَّى جَعَلْناهُمْ ححصِيداً خامِدِينَ — 'They said, "Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers." So that remained their cry until We made them harvested, extinguished.' By Allah, this is an exhortation for you and a frightening — if you take heed and fear."

Ayahs

وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ (١٦)
We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them for play. (16)
لَوْ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نَّتَّخِذَ لَهْوًا لَّٱتَّخَذْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّآ إِن كُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ (١٧)
Had We desired to take up some diversion We would have taken it up with Ourselves, were We to do [so]. (17)
بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِٱلْحَقِّ عَلَى ٱلْبَـٰطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُۥ فَإِذَا هُوَ زَاهِقٌ ۚ وَلَكُمُ ٱلْوَيْلُ مِمَّا تَصِفُونَ (١٨)
Indeed, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and behold, falsehood vanishes! And woe to you for what you allege [about Allah]. (18)
وَلَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَنْ عِندَهُۥ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ (١٩)
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and those who are near Him do not disdain to worship Him, nor do they become weary. (19)
يُسَبِّحُونَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ لَا يَفْتُرُونَ (٢٠)
They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag. (20)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ هُمْ يُنشِرُونَ (٢١)
Have they taken gods from the earth who raise [the dead]? (21)
لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَآ ءَالِهَةٌ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتَا ۚ فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (٢٢)
Had there been any gods in them other than Allah, they would surely have fallen apart. Clear is Allah, the Lord of the Throne, of what they allege [concerning Him]. (22)
لَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْـَٔلُونَ (٢٣)
He is not questioned concerning what He does, but they are questioned. (23)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً ۖ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ ۖ هَـٰذَا ذِكْرُ مَن مَّعِىَ وَذِكْرُ مَن قَبْلِى ۗ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ فَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٢٤)
Have they taken gods besides Him? Say, ‘Produce your evidence! This is a precept of those who are with me, and a precept of those [who went] before me.’ But most of them do not know the truth, and so they are disregardful. (24)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدُونِ (٢٥)
We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me.’ (25)
وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۚ بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ (٢٦)
They say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken offsprings.’ Immaculate is He! Indeed, they are [His] honoured servants. (26)
لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِۦ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٧)
They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command. (27)
يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ٱرْتَضَىٰ وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِۦ مُشْفِقُونَ (٢٨)
He knows that which is before them and that which is behind them, and they do not intercede except for someone He approves of, and they are apprehensive for the fear of Him. (28)
۞ وَمَن يَقُلْ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّىٓ إِلَـٰهٌ مِّن دُونِهِۦ فَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِيهِ جَهَنَّمَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٩)
Should any of them say, ‘I am a god besides Him,’ We will requite him with hell. Thus do We requite the wrongdoers. (29)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًا فَفَتَقْنَـٰهُمَا ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ حَىٍّ ۖ أَفَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٠)
Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were interwoven and We unravelled them, and We made every living thing out of water? Will they not then have faith? (30)
وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا فِجَاجًا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (٣١)
We set firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with them, and We made broad ways in them so that they may be guided [to their destinations]. (31)
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ سَقْفًا مَّحْفُوظًا ۖ وَهُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهَا مُعْرِضُونَ (٣٢)
We made the sky a preserved roof and yet they are disregardful of its signs. (32)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ (٣٣)
It is He who created the night and the day, the sun and the moon, each swimming in an orbit. (33)

(Commentary)

The opening of these verses justifies the descent of punishment upon the unjust towns by negating the notion of "play" (اللعب) in creation. It asserts that Allah did not amuse Himself (لم يله) by bringing into existence the heavens, the earth, and all that lies between them, such that people would be left to their own desires, doing as they please and playing however they wish without being held accountable for their deeds. Rather, they were created only to return to their Lord and be called to account.

Thus, they shall be recompensed according to their actions; for they are servants (عباد) who are responsible (مسئولون). If they overstep the bounds of servitude (العبودية), they are seized by that which divine wisdom (الحكمة الإلهية) necessitates—and indeed, Allah is ever on the watch.

Since this explanation is itself an argument for the Resurrection (المعاد) — the return of all beings to Allah for judgment and recompense — the discourse transitions to it, and the argument for it is established. Through the establishment of the Resurrection, Prophethood (النبوة) — the divine office of guidance through revelation — is also proven, because Prophethood is among the necessary implications of the obligation of servitude, which in turn is a necessary implication of the reality of the Resurrection. Thus, the first two verses serve as a link between the preceding and succeeding contexts.

The verses contain an exquisite exposition for proving the Resurrection, addressing and negating all possibilities that contradict it, as you shall see.

His saying, the Exalted: وما خلقنا السماء والأرض وما بينهما لاعبين لو أردنا أن نتخذ لهوا لاتخذناه من لدنا إن كنا فاعلين — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. Had We desired to take up a diversion, We would have taken it up from those near Us, were We to do [such a thing]. (21:16-17). These two verses justify the descent of punishment upon the unjust towns whose destruction Allah, the Glorified, previously mentioned. According to the preceding context, they are also a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) for the reality of the Resurrection, and subsequently for Prophethood, which is the primary objective of the discourse in this Surah.

The sum of what has preceded is that there is a Resurrection in which the deeds of people will be accounted for. Therefore, it is necessary that they distinguish between good and evil, and between righteous deeds (صالح الأعمال) and corrupt ones, through divine guidance (هداية إلهية). This guidance is the true call based on Prophethood; were it not for this, creation would be in vain (عبثا), and Allah, the Glorified, would be a "player" (لاعبا) amusing Himself with it—exalted is He far above that.

The status of these two verses—as you can see—is that of arguing for the truth of the Resurrection so that the truth of the prophetic call may be established through it. This is because the prophetic call, in this sense, is a requirement of the Resurrection, and not the other way around.

The argument in these two verses relies on the meanings of "play" (اللعب) and "diversion" (اللهو). Play (اللعب) is an organized action that has an imaginary, non-real goal, such as the games of children, which have no effect except for imaginary concepts like being ahead or behind, winning or losing, and benefit or harm—all of which exist only by assumption and fancy. Since play is something the soul is attracted to, it distracts it from real, purposeful actions; thus, it is one of the instances of diversion (اللهو).

If the creation of the witnessed world were not for a goal toward which it is directed and intended, and if Allah, the Glorified, were to continually bring into existence and annihilate, give life and cause death, build and destroy, not for a goal resulting from these acts nor for a purpose for which He does what He does, but rather did them for their own sake, wanting to see them one after another, occupying Himself with them to ward off boredom, weariness, or laziness, or to flee from loneliness or escape seclusion—as is our case when we engage in an act of play or diversion to repel a deficiency that has occurred to us or an unpleasant state we do not like, such as boredom, fatigue, laziness, failure, and the like—[then that would be play].

Play, from another perspective, is diversion (لهو). For this reason, we see that the Exalted expressed it in the first verse as play: وما خلقنا السماء والأرض وما بينهما لاعبين (We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play), then replaced it in the second verse—which serves as its justification—with diversion (لهوا). He placed "diversion" in the stead of "play" to complete the argument.

His being diverted (تلهيه), the Exalted, by something from His creation is impossible (محال), because diversion only becomes diversion by fulfilling a need of the one diverted or by repelling a deficiency in his own self. Thus, it would be among the influential causes (الأسباب المؤثرة). There is no meaning to His creation influencing Him, nor to His needing that which is needy of Him in every respect. Therefore, if one were to assume His being diverted by a diversion, it could not be something external to His self (نفسه). His creation is His act, and His act is external to His self; rather, it would have to be something not external to His essence (ذاته).

With this, the proof is completed that Allah did not create the heavens, the earth, and what is between them for play or diversion, nor did He originate them in vain and without a goal or purpose. This is His saying: لو أردنا أن نتخذ لهوا لاتخذناه من لدنا (Had We desired to take up a diversion, We would have taken it up from those near Us).

As for diversion through something not external to His essence, although it is impossible in itself—as it would imply a need in His essence for something to occupy Him and distract Him from what He finds in Himself, making His essence composed of a real, established need and something that fulfills that need—and there is no way for deficiency or need to reach His Transcendent Essence; nevertheless, the proof does not depend on this. This is because the context is to explain that there is no play or diversion in His act, which is His creation. As for the fact that there is no play or diversion in His Essence, that is outside the purpose of this context. This possibility was merely alluded to by the use of the word "if" (لو), which denotes impossibility (الامتناع), and then emphasized by His saying: إن كنا فاعلين (were We to do [such a thing]). Understand this well.

Through this explanation, it becomes clear that His saying: لو أردنا (Had We desired...) etc., is in the position of justifying the negation in His saying: وما خلقنا (We did not create...) etc. Furthermore, His saying: من لدنا (from those near Us) means "from Ourself," in the stage of the Essence (الذات) rather than the stage of creation (الخلق), which is Our act external to Our Essence. His saying: إن كنا فاعلين (were We to do [such a thing]) is an independent allusion to what the word "if" (لو) indicates within the sentence, serving as a type of emphasis.

With this exposition, the proof for the Resurrection and then Prophethood is completed, and the discourse connects to the preceding context. Its summary is that for mankind there is a return to Allah and an accounting for their deeds to be recompensed with reward or punishment. Therefore, it is necessary that there be Prophethood and a call to guide them toward the beliefs and actions for which they will be recompensed. Thus, the Resurrection is the purpose of creation that necessitates Prophethood. If there were no Resurrection, there would be no purpose or goal for creation, and creation would be play and diversion on His part, which is impermissible. Even if it were permissible for Him to take up diversion, it would have to be through something not external to His self, not through creation, which is an act external to His Essence. This is because it is impossible for anything else to influence Him or for Him to need anything else in any way. Since creation is not play, there is a goal, which is the Resurrection, and that necessitates Prophethood. Among its implications is also the exemplary punishment (نكال) of some wrongdoers when they transgress and exceed bounds, and the revival of the truth depends on this, as alluded to by His subsequent saying: بل نقذف بالحق على الباطل فيدمغه فإذا هو زاهق (Rather We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and behold, it vanishes).

The commentators have several views regarding the interpretation of His saying: لو أردنا أن نتخذ لهوا لاتخذناه من لدنا:

Among them is what is mentioned in al-Kashshaf, the summary of which is that His saying: من لدنا (from those near Us) means "by Our power" (بقدرتنا). Thus, the meaning is: "If We wished to take up a diversion, We would have taken it up by Our power, due to its universality, but We do not wish it." This is indicated by the word "if" (لو) denoting impossibility.

The critique of this is that power (القدرة) does not relate to the impossible. Diversion—meaning that which distracts you from what concerns you in any way—is impossible for Him, the Exalted. Furthermore, the indication of "from those near Us" (من لدنا) toward "power" is not without obscurity.

Another view is that of some who said: The meaning of His saying: من لدنا is "from Our presence" such that no one would be aware of it, because it is a deficiency, and concealing it would be more appropriate.

The critique of this is that concealing a deficiency is only out of fear of blame, and fear is only for one who is not free from the mark of incapacity (العجز), not for Him Who is powerful over all things. If He were to remove one deficiency through diversion, He would remove another through what is appropriate. Moreover, if it were impossible for Him to manifest it because it is a deficiency, then its original impossibility for Him because it is a deficiency precedes the impossibility of manifesting it. Thus, the meaning would boil down to: "If We were to do this impossible thing, We would hide it from you because manifesting it is impossible," which is as you can see [absurd].

Another view is that of some who said that "diversion" (لهو) refers to a wife and child. The Arabs call a wife "diversion" and a child "diversion" because one finds rest (يستروح) in them, and diversion is that which gives rest to the soul. Thus, the meaning is: "If We wanted to take a companion and a child—or one of them—We would have taken them from those near to Us." This is like His saying: لو أراد الله أن يتخذ ولدا لاصطفى مما يخلق ما يشاء — Had Allah intended to take a son, He would have chosen from what He creates whatever He wished. (39:4).

It was also said: "We would have taken it from the high immaterial beings (المجردات العالية), not from the low bodies and corporeal things." It was also said: "We would have taken it from the wide-eyed houris." In any case, this is a refutation of those like the Christians who affirm a companion and a child, namely Mary and the Messiah (peace be upon him).

The critique of this is that even if it were correct linguistically, it would necessitate a break in the discourse from the preceding context.

Another view from some is that the meaning of His saying: من لدنا is "from Our side." The meaning of the verse would be: "If We wanted to take up a diversion, it would be a diversion from Our side, i.e., a divine diversion, which is the wisdom that you have taken as diversion from your side." This would be the essence of seriousness and wisdom. Thus, it is in the sense of "If We wanted it, it would be impossible." Its summary is that His side, the Exalted, accepts nothing but seriousness and wisdom; so if He wanted diversion, it would become seriousness and wisdom—meaning it is impossible for diversion to be intended by Him, the Exalted.

The critique of this is that although it is a correct meaning in itself and not lacking in subtlety, it is not understood from the wording of the verse, as is apparent.

Regarding His saying: إن كنا فاعلين (were We to do [such a thing]), it is apparent that "if" (إن) is conditional, as previously alluded to. On this basis, its apodosis (جزاؤه) is omitted, indicated by His saying: لاتخذناه من لدنا. Some said that "if" (إن) is negatory (نافية), and the sentence is the result of the previous explanation. It is narrated from some that the negatory "if" (إن النافية) is usually accompanied by the distinguishing "lam" (اللام الفارقة). It has become clear from the preceding meaning of the verse that "if" being conditional is more eloquent according to the context than it being negatory.

His saying, the Exalted: بل نقذف بالحق على الباطل فيدمغه فإذا هو زاهق ولكم الويل مما تصفون — Rather We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and behold, it vanishes! And woe to you for what you allege. (21:18). Hurl (القذف) is to throw from a distance. Crushing the head (الدمغ)—according to Majma' al-Bayan—is to fracture the head until it reaches the brain; it is said "he crushed him" (دمغه) when he hits his brain. The vanishing of the soul (زهوق النفس) is its destruction and perishing; it is said "the thing vanished" (زهق الشيء) meaning it perished.

Truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل) are two opposite concepts. Truth (الحق) is that which has a fixed reality (الثابت العين), while falsehood (الباطل) is that which has no fixed reality but resembles the truth so much that it is thought to be it, until they clash, whereupon the truth remains and falsehood vanishes. This is like water, which is one of the realities (الحقائق), and a mirage (السراب), which is not truly water but resembles it in the eye of the beholder, so the thirsty one thinks it is water until he reaches it and finds it to be nothing.

The Glorified has cited many examples of truth and falsehood in His speech. He considered beliefs that correspond to reality to be truth, and those that do not to be falsehood. He considered the Hereafter to be truth, and the life of this world—with all that man sees for himself in it and strives for, such as kingdom, wealth, status, children, and helpers—to be falsehood. He considered His Transcendent Essence to be truth, and all other causes by which man is deceived and upon which he relies besides Allah to be falsehood. The verses regarding this are many, and there is no room to cite them here.

That which is attributed to Him, the Exalted, primarily is the truth, not falsehood, as He said: الحق من ربك — The truth is from your Lord (3:60), and He said: وما خلقنا السماء والأرض وما بينهما باطلا — We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain (38:27). As for falsehood, insofar as it is falsehood, it is not attributed to Him directly; rather, it is a necessary consequence of the deficiency of some things when the deficient is compared to the perfect. False beliefs are consequences of the deficiency of perception, and other false matters are consequences of things when compared to what is more perfect than them. They are attributed to Him, the Exalted, by permission (بالإذن), in the sense that His creation of the salty, reflective earth such that it appears to the beholder in the color and clarity of water is a permission from Him, the Exalted, for water to be imagined there—which is the realization of the mirage as an imaginary, false realization.

From here it becomes clear that there is nothing in existence but that it has a mixture of falsehood, except for Allah, the Glorified; for He is the Truth (الحق) with which no falsehood is mixed and to which falsehood has no path. He said: أن الله هو الحق — that Allah is the Manifest Truth (24:25).

It also becomes clear that creation, despite the order within it, is a mixture of truth and falsehood. The Exalted said, exemplifying the matter of creation: أنزل من السماء ماء فسالت أودية بقدرها فاحتمل السيل زبدا رابيا ومما يوقدون عليه في النار ابتغاء حلية أو متاع زبد مثله كذلك يضرب الله الحق والباطل فأما الزبد فيذهب جفاء وأما ما ينفع الناس فيمكث في الأرض — He sends down water from the sky, and the valleys flow according to their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire, seeking ornament or utility, comes a similar scum. Thus does Allah exemplify the truth and the falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, but as for what benefits mankind, it remains in the earth. (13:17). Beneath this lies vast knowledge.

The way (سنة) of Allah, the Exalted, has proceeded such that He gives respite to falsehood until, when it opposes the truth to nullify it and take its place, He hurls the truth at it, and behold, it vanishes. Thus, the true belief is never uprooted, even if its carriers are few at times or weakened. True perfection does not perish from its root, even if its opposites multiply. Divine victory does not bypass His messengers, even if they might reach a point where they despair and think they have been lied to.

This is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: بل نقذف بالحق على الباطل فيدمغه فإذا هو زاهق. It is a transition (إضراب) from the negation of creating the world for play, or from the negation of the desire to take up diversion indicated by His saying: لو أردنا أن نتخذ لهوا etc. In His saying: نقذف (We hurl), which implies continuity, there is an indication that this is a prevailing way (سنة جارية). In His saying: نقذف ... فيدمغه (We hurl... and it crushes its head), there is an indication of the superiority of truth over falsehood. In His saying: فإذا هو زاهق (and behold, it vanishes), there is an indication of the suddenness and unexpectedness of the hurling, at a time when no victory for the truth or defeat for falsehood is expected. The verse is absolute, not restricted to truth and falsehood in argument, or in conduct and custom, or in creation; there is no evidence to restrict it to any of that.

The meaning is: We did not create the world for play, nor did We desire to take up diversion; rather, Our way is to hurl the truth against falsehood from a distance so that it destroys it, and it is suddenly gone and perished. If the falsehood is an argument or a belief, the argument of truth nullifies it. If it is an action or a custom, as in the case of the extravagant, unjust towns, then the uprooting punishment eradicates and nullifies it. If it is something else, then it is something else.

Some have interpreted the verse by saying: "But We do not desire to take up diversion; rather, Our affair is to overcome the truth—which includes seriousness—over falsehood—which includes diversion." This is an error, for it contains an admission of the existence of diversion, whereas nothing was mentioned in the preceding discourse except the diversion attributed to Him, the Exalted, which Allah negated from Himself. The truth is that the verse is not absolute regarding seriousness and diversion, as diversion does not exist [in His act] for the verse to include it and its opposite.

His saying: ولكم الويل مما تصفون (And woe to you for what you allege) is a threat to the people who deny the Resurrection and Prophethood, according to the previous clarification of the context's requirements.

From the verse, the reality of the return to Allah, the Exalted, becomes apparent: He continually hurls the truth against falsehood, thereby establishing the truth and purifying it from the falsehood that mixes with it or covers it, until nothing remains but the Pure Truth, which is Allah, the Truth, exalted is His Name. He said: ويعلمون أن الله هو الحق المبين — and they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth (24:25). On that day, the perceived independent influence of causes will fall away, and the power, kingdom, and command claimed for others will vanish, as He said: لقد تقطع بينكم وضل عنكم ما كنتم تزعمون — Certainly the ties between you have been severed, and what you used to assert has forsaken you (6:94), and He said: أن القوة لله جميعا — that power belongs entirely to Allah (2:165), and He said: لمن الملك اليوم لله الواحد القهار — ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (40:16), and He said: والأمر يومئذ لله — and the command that day will belong to Allah (82:19). The verses indicating this meaning are many.

His saying, the Exalted: وله من في السماوات والأرض — To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth (21:19). This refutes one of the possibilities that contradict the Resurrection in general, which is that He, the Glorified, might not have authority over some or all people, such that those He does not own would escape returning to Him, the accounting, and the recompense. The answer is that His ownership (ملكه), the Exalted, is general and encompasses everyone in the heavens and the earth; thus, He has the right to dispose of them in whatever way He wills.

It is well known that this ownership is real (حقيقي), a necessary consequence of origination (الإيجاد), in the sense of a thing's subsistence through its originating cause such that it cannot disobey it in any disposal. Origination is exclusive to Allah, the Glorified; no one else shares it with Him, even according to the idolaters who affirm other gods for management and worship. Thus, everyone in the heavens and the earth is owned by Allah; there is no owner besides Him.

His saying, the Exalted: ومن عنده لا يستكبرون عن عبادته ولا يستحسرون — and those who are with Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they grow weary (21:19), until the end of the next verse. It is said in Majma' al-Bayan: Growing weary (الاستحسار) is to stop due to exhaustion; it is said "a weary camel" (بعير حسير) meaning exhausted. Its root is from their saying: "he bared (حسر) his arms," so the meaning is that he revealed his strength through exhaustion. End quote.

The meaning of His saying: ومن عنده (and those who are with Him) refers to those distinguished by the gift of proximity and presence, and it often applies to the archangels (الملائكة المقربون). His saying: يسبحون الليل والنهار لا يفترون — They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag (21:20) is in the position of explaining His saying: ولا يستحسرون (nor do they grow weary), meaning they are not overtaken by exhaustion or fatigue; rather, they glorify Him night and day without flagging. Glorifying by night and day is a metaphor for the continuity of glorification without interruption.

He, the Exalted, describes the state of those near to Him among His servants and the honored ones among His angels: they are immersed in His servitude, devoted to His worship; no preoccupation distracts them from it, nor does any diverter turn them away. It is as if the discourse is intended to explain the specific nature of His ownership and sovereignty mentioned at the beginning of the verse.

This is because the prevailing custom between masters and their slaves in conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري) is that the more a slave increases in proximity to his master, the more the master lightens his burden by overlooking many of the duties and protocols applicable to the general slaves, and he is excused from accounting and punishment. This is because human social life is built on cooperation through the exchange of benefits according to the touch of need. Need is always present, and the master is more in need of his close slaves than others, just as a king is more in need of those close to his presence than others. If the master's benefit from his close slave is greater than from others, then the honor he bestows in exchange for the benefits of his service should be likewise. Therefore, much of what is imposed on others is lifted from him, and he is excused from some of what others are held accountable for; for it is merely a transaction and a trade.

This is in contrast to His ownership, the Exalted, of His servants, for it is a real ownership (ملك حقيقي) in which the Owner is in absolute self-sufficiency (غنى مطلق) from His owned, and the owned is in absolute need of his Owner. The situation does not change with proximity or distance, or high or low status. Rather, the more the servant increases in proximity, the more the greatness, majesty, might, and splendor become apparent to him, and the more his sense of his own soul's lowliness, poverty, and need increases, necessitating further immersion in the humility of servitude and the submissiveness of worship.

Thus, His saying: ومن عنده لا يستكبرون عن عبادته ولا يستحسرون etc., is an indication that His ownership, the Exalted—which He previously indicated necessitates worship, accounting, and recompense—is contrary to the ownership prevalent in human society. Therefore, let no one hope to be excused from action, accounting, or recompense.

It is possible that the sentence is in the position of progression (الترقي), and the meaning is: To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth, so they must worship and will be held to account without exception, to the extent that those with Him among His close servants and honored angels are not too proud to worship Him nor do they grow weary; rather, they glorify Him with a continuous, uninterrupted glorification.

It has already been derived at the end of Surah al-A'raf, in the interpretation of His saying: إن الذين عند ربك لا يستكبرون عن عبادته ويسبحونه وله يسجدون — Indeed those who are with your Lord are not too proud to worship Him: they glorify Him and prostrate to Him (7:206), that the meaning of الذين عند ربك (those who are with your Lord) is more general than just the archangels; so do not be unmindful.

His saying, the Exalted: أم اتخذوا آلهة من الأرض هم ينشرون — Have they taken gods from the earth who raise [the dead]? (21:21). Raising (الإنشار) is the reviving of the dead, so it refers to the Resurrection. In the verse is the refutation of another possibility that contradicts the Resurrection and the previously mentioned accounting—namely, the return to Allah—by saying: "There are other gods besides Allah who raise the dead and account them, and Allah, the Glorified, has nothing to do with the matter of the Resurrection such that we should fear Him and be forced to answer His messengers and follow them in their call; rather, we worship them [the other gods] and there is no harm."

The restriction of His saying: أم اتخذوا آلهة (Have they taken gods) with His saying: من الأرض (from the earth) was said to indicate that if they are from the earth, their ruling is the ruling of all the people of the earth—death and then resurrection; so who is it that causes them to die and then raises them?

It is also possible that the meaning is taking gods from the genus of the earth, such as idols made of stone, wood, and metals. In this, there would be a type of sarcasm and belittlement. The meaning would boil down to: If the angels, who are gods in their view, are among His servants and slaves, and these people are cut off from them and have lost hope in their divinity to resort to them in the matter of the Resurrection, will they then take their idols and statues as gods besides Allah in place of the lords of the idols and statues?

His saying, the Exalted: لو كان فيهما آلهة إلا الله لفسدت فسبحان الله رب العرش عما يصفون — Had there been any gods in them other than Allah, they would surely have fallen apart. Clear is Allah, the Lord of the Throne, of what they allege. (21:22).
