It has already been mentioned in the interpretation of Surah Hud, and the indication was repeated after it, that the dispute between the idolaters and the monotheists is not regarding the oneness or multiplicity of the "God" (الإله) in the sense of the Necessary Being (الواجب الوجود) who exists by His essence and originates others. This is something in which there is no dispute that He is One, without partner. Rather, the dispute is regarding the "God" (الإله) in the sense of the Lord (الرب), the Worshipped (المعبود). The idolaters hold that the management (تدبير) of the world, across its various levels and parts, is delegated to noble beings who are near to Allah and should be worshipped so that they may intercede for their worshippers with Allah and bring them closer to Him in station—such as the lord of the heaven, the lord of the earth, the lord of man, and so on. These are gods besides Him, while Allah, the Glorified, is the God of gods and the Creator of all, as He narrates from them: ولئن سألتهم من خلقهم ليقولن الله — If you ask them, ‘Who created them?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ (43:87), and His saying: ولئن سألتهم من خلق السماوات والأرض ليقولن خلقهن العزيز العليم — If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘The All-mighty, the All-knowing created them.’ (43:9).

The noble verse only negates gods besides Allah in the heaven and the earth in this sense, not in the sense of the Originating Maker (الصانع الموجد), whose multiplicity no one asserts. The meaning of the God being "in the heaven and the earth" is the attachment of His divinity to the heaven and the earth, not His dwelling in them. It is like His saying: هو الذي في السماء إله وفي الأرض إله — It is He who is God in the sky and God on the earth (43:84).

The formulation of the verse's argument is that if one were to assume gods for the world more than one, they would be different in essence (ذاتا) and distinct in reality (حقيقة). The distinction of their realities would necessitate the distinction of their management (تدبيرهم), and thus the managements would conflict and the heaven and the earth would fall apart (لفسدتا). However, the prevailing order is a single order, whose parts are harmonious in their goals. Therefore, there are no gods for the world more than one, and He is the sought One.

If you say: The occurrence of corruption (الفساد) is sufficiently witnessed in the clashing of causes and means (تزاحم الأسباب والعلل), and their clashing in their influence on matter is itself corruption.

I say: The conflict of two causes under two managements is different from their conflict under a single management, which limits the effect of each to produce a specific result. What exists of the clashing of causes in the order is of this [latter] type. For the various causes and means that delineate this general order, despite their differences, mutual prevention, and clashing, do not nullify the efficacy of one another in the sense of some universal laws governing the order being abrogated by others, such that they would fail in their application when conditions are met and obstacles removed. This [abrogation] is what is meant by the management of one manager corrupting the work of another. Rather, the two different, contending causes are like the two scales of a balance, contending in rising and falling; in the very essence of their difference, they are united in achieving what the owner of the balance intends, serving his purpose, which is the adjustment of weight by means of the needle.

If you say: The traces of knowledge and consciousness are witnessed in the order prevailing in the universe. Thus, the Managing Lord (الرب المدبر) manages it through knowledge. If this is so, why is it not possible to assume gods more than one who manage the affair of the universe through intellectual management (تدبيرا تعقليا), having agreed not to differ or hinder one another in their management to preserve the interest (المصلحة)?

I say: This is not intelligible. For the meaning of intellectual management for us is that we apply our actions proceeding from us to what the intellectual laws necessitate—those laws that preserve the harmony of the parts of the action and its progression toward its goal. These intellectual laws are taken from external realities and the order prevailing in them that governs them. Thus, our intellectual actions are subordinate to the intellectual laws, which are subordinate to the external order. But the Lord Who manages the universe—His act is the very external order itself, which is the object of subordination for the intellectual laws. It is impossible for His act to be subordinate to the intellectual laws while it is the object of subordination. Understand this well.

This is the formulation of the verse's argument, and it is a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) composed of certain premises (مقدمات يقينية) indicating that the general management as it proceeds—with the specific managements it contains and is composed of—originates from a single, non-differing source. However, the commentators formulated it as an argument for negating the multiplicity of the Maker (الصانع) and differed in its formulation. Some added premises to it that are outside the wording of the verse, and they delved into it until some said it is a persuasive argument (حجة إقناعية), not a demonstrative one, mentioned only to persuade the common folk.

His saying, the Exalted: فسبحان الله رب العرش عما يصفون (Clear is Allah, the Lord of the Throne, of what they allege) is a sanctification of Him, the Exalted, from their description—which is that there are gods with Him who raise the dead, or that there are gods besides Him who own the management in His kingdom. The Throne (العرش) is a metaphor for the kingdom (الملك). In His saying: عما يصفون, the "ma" (ما) is infinitive (مصدرية), and the meaning is: from their describing.

There is a completion to this discourse that will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يسئل عما يفعل وهم يسئلون — He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned. (21:23). The pronoun in لا يسئل (He is not questioned) refers to Him, the Exalted, without doubt. The pronoun in وهم يسئلون (they are questioned) refers to the gods they call upon, or to the gods and people together, or to the people only. The best of these views is the first, because it is appropriate to the context, as the discourse is about the gods they call upon besides Him. They are the ones questioned, while Allah, the Glorified, is not questioned about His act.

Questioning an act is our saying to its doer: "Why did you do such and such?" It is a question about the aspect of interest (المصلحة) in the act. For an act accompanied by interest is not subject to blame among the rational. Since Allah is Wise (حكيم) in an absolute sense, as He described Himself in various places of His speech, and the Wise is He who does not perform an act except for a preponderant interest, there is naturally no meaning to questioning His act. This is in contrast to others, for it is possible in their case to do truth or falsehood, and for their act to be accompanied by interest or corruption (المفسدة). Thus, questioning is permissible in their case so that they may be held accountable by rational blame or masterly punishment if the act is not accompanied by interest.

This is what a group of commentators mentioned in justifying the verse, and it is a correct meaning in general, but two matters remain:

The first matter: The verse is absolute (مطلقة); there is no evidence in its wording that this specific meaning is intended. The fact that a meaning is correct in itself does not necessitate that it is the intended meaning of the verse.

For this reason, some justified the lack of questioning by basing it on the fact that Allah's acts are not caused by purposes (الأغراض). This is because a purpose is what impels the doer to the act so that he may become perfect through it and benefit from it. Since He, the Exalted, is too majestic to need anything external to His Essence or to become perfect through benefiting from another, it is not said to Him: "Why did you do such and such?" as a question about the purpose that impelled Him to the act.

This is refuted by the fact that a doer who is complete in his efficacy (الفاعلية) only performs the act for his essence; thus, his essence is his goal and purpose in his act, without needing a purpose external to his essence. This is like a generous person who gives much so that he may acquire the trait of generosity; once the trait is acquired, the giving proceeds from it for its own sake, not to acquire what is already present. Thus, the trait itself is its own goal in its act.

For this reason also, others justified the lack of questioning in the verse by saying that His greatness, majesty, might, and splendor, the Exalted, overwhelm everything such that no one can question Him about His act or object to Him in any of the affairs of His will. Others besides Him, the Exalted, are too lowly and insignificant to dare to question Him or hold Him accountable for an act. However, He, the Glorified, has the right to question every doer about his act and hold accountable everyone who deserves accountability.

This is refuted by the fact that the lack of questioning due to there being no one capable of questioning Him out of fear of His power and wrath—like tyrannical kings and arrogant despots—is different from the act being such that it is not marked by deficiency or flagging, nor afflicted by flaw or shortcoming. That which the general body of His speech, the Exalted, indicates is that His act is of the second type, not the first, such as His saying: الذي أحسن كل شيء خلقه — who perfected everything He created (32:7), and His saying: له الأسماء الحسنى — To Him belong the Best Names (59:24), and His saying: إن الله لا يظلم الناس شيئا — Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least (10:44), among other verses.

In summary, their saying that He is only not questioned about His act because He is Wise in an absolute sense boils down to the fact that the lack of questioning about His act is not due to the essence of His act as an act, but due to something external to the essence of the act—namely, that its doer is Wise and does nothing except what contains a preponderant interest. Yet His saying: لا يسئل عما يفعل وهم يسئلون has no indication in its wording of restriction to wisdom; thus, they ought to have provided evidence for it.

If it were permissible to go beyond the wording of the verse in justifying the lack of questioning, it would be closer to hold onto His saying—which is connected to the verse—: فسبحان الله رب العرش عما يصفون. For the verse establishes for Him absolute ownership (الملك المطلق), and the King is followed in His will and obeyed in His command because He is King—i.e., by His essence—not because His act or word corresponds to a preponderant interest. Otherwise, there would be no difference between Him and the lowest of His subjects, and the interest would be the object of following, and His obedience would not be obligatory at times. Similarly, the master is followed and obeyed by his slave in the mastership he possesses from the aspect that he is a master; the slave has no right to question him regarding what he wants from him and commands him about the aspect of wisdom and interest. Thus, ownership, with all its breadth, is the principle for the permissibility of disposals and sovereignty over them by its essence.

So Allah, the Glorified, is the King and Owner of all, and all are purely owned by Him. He has the right to do what He wills and judge as He intends, and no one else has that right. He has the right to question them about what they do, and no one else has the right to question Him about what He does. Yes, He, the Glorified, has informed us that He is Wise, doing nothing except what contains an interest and wanting nothing but that. Thus, we have no right to think ill of Him in what is attributed to Him of action after this general knowledge of His absolute wisdom, let alone question Him about what He does. Among the most subtle verses indicating what we have mentioned is His saying, narrating from Jesus son of Mary: إن تعذبهم فإنهم عبادك وإن تغفر لهم فإنك أنت العزيز الحكيم — If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise. (5:118), where he justifies their punishment by their being owned by Him, and justifies their forgiveness by His being Wise.

From here it appears that wisdom, in a certain way, is more general than His saying: لا يسئل عما يفعل, unlike ownership. Thus, ownership is closer to justifying the verse than wisdom, as we have indicated.

The second matter: The verse, according to how they justified it, has an obscure connection to the preceding context. The most that has been said regarding its connection to what precedes it is what is in Majma' al-Bayan: that when He explained monotheism (التوحيد), He followed it with the explanation of justice (العدل). You are well aware that this boils down to digression (استطراد), for which there is no necessity.

Similar to this is what is narrated from Abu Muslim, that it connects to His saying at the beginning of the Surah: اقترب للناس حسابهم — The time of people’s reckoning has drawn near (21:1). Reckoning (الحساب) is the questioning about what Allah bestowed upon them: did they meet His blessings with gratitude or with ingratitude? The critique of this is that the verses following this verse have a clear connection to what precedes them; thus, there is no meaning to it alone connecting to the beginning of the Surah. Furthermore, his view, even if accepted, would only justify the connection of the end of the verse, while the beginning remains as it was.

You are well aware that justifying the verse through ownership rather than wisdom, as we have presented, reveals the connection of the verse to what precedes it in His saying: فسبحان الله رب العرش عما يصفون. The Throne (العرش)—as mentioned—is a metaphor for ownership. Thus, the two verses are connected, and His saying: لا يسئل عما يفعل وهم يسئلون is in reality a proof for His ownership, the Exalted, just as His ownership and lack of accountability are a proof for His Lordship (ربوبيته). It is also a proof for their being owned, just as their being owned and their accountability are a proof for their lack of Lordship. For the doer who is not questioned about his act in any way is the one who owns the act absolutely, without doubt. And the doer who is questioned about his act is the one who does not own the act except when it possesses an interest, and the interest is what owns him and removes accountability from him. The Lord of the world, or a part of its parts, is the one who owns its management independently by his essence—i.e., for his essence—not by the gift of another. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of the Throne, and others are nurtured (مربوبون) by Him.

( A Discussion on His Wisdom, the Exalted, and the Meaning of His Act being Accompanied by Interest )

This is a Philosophical and Quranic Discussion

The various and different movements that proceed from us are only considered an "act" of ours if they are attached in some way to our will (إرادتنا). Thus, health, sickness, and involuntary movement—such as the daily or annual movement [of the earth]—are not considered acts of ours. It is necessary that the will for an act follows the knowledge of its preponderance (رجحانه) and the conviction of its being a perfection (كمال) for us, in the sense that doing it is better than leaving it, and its benefit generally outweighs its harm. That which is in the act from the aspect of the good resulting from it is what makes it preponderant—i.e., it is what impels us toward the act, meaning it is the cause for the efficacy of the doer among us. This is what we call the goal (غاية) of the doer in his act and his purpose (غرضه) from his act. Philosophical investigations have concluded that an act—in the sense of the effect proceeding from the doer, whether voluntary or involuntary—is never devoid of a goal.

The act's inclusion of an aspect of goodness resulting from its realization is what is called the interest (مصلحة) of the act. The interest that the rational—who are the people of human society—consider an interest is what impels the doer to his act, and it is the cause for the perfection of the act that necessitates the doer being considered wise (حكيما) in his act. Were it not for it, the act would be in vain (لغوا), having no effect.

It is necessary that the interest resulting from the act has no existence before the existence of the act. Thus, its being an impeller for the doer toward the act and a caller for him to it is only by its existence in knowledge (علما), not by its existence externally (خارجا). This means that one of us has a mental image (صورة علمية) taken from the external order, with its prevailing universal laws and organized principles governing the progression of movements to their goals and acts to their purposes, and what he has acquired through experience of the connections of things with one another. There is no doubt that this mental order is subordinate to the external order and results from it.

The role of the voluntary doer among us is to apply his specific movements, called an "act," to the mental order he possesses and to observe the interests established within it in his act by building his will upon them. If he succeeds in applying the act to the knowledge, he is wise in his act and perfect in his work. If he errs in the correspondence of the knowledge to the external object of knowledge, or if he fails due to incapacity or negligence, he is not called wise, but rather vain, ignorant, or the like.

Thus, wisdom (الحكمة) is an attribute of the doer from the aspect of his act's correspondence to the mental order that corresponds to the external order, and his act's inclusion of interest is its resulting from the mental image that results from the external world. So wisdom is, in reality, an essential attribute of the external world, and the doer or his act is only described by it from the aspect of the act's correspondence to it by means of knowledge. Similarly, the act includes interest in the sense of its branching from its mental image that mimics the external world.

This is only completed in an act intended to correspond to the external world, like our voluntary acts. As for the act which is the very external world itself—which is the act of Allah, the Glorified—it is the very essence of wisdom, not because it mimics another thing that is wisdom. His act includes interest in the sense that it is the object of subordination for interest, not subordinate to interest such that it would call Him to it and impel Him toward it, as you have learned.

Every doer besides Him, the Exalted, is questioned about his act with the question: "Why did you do such and such?" The objective of this is that he apply his act to the external order through the mental order he possesses and point to the aspect of interest that impelled him toward the act. As for Him, the Glorified, there is no place for questioning His act, because His act is the very external order itself to which the question seeks to apply the act; there is no other external order for Him to apply it to. His act is that whose mental image is an interest that calls and impels toward the act, and there is no other order above it—as you have heard—for the mental image taken from it to be an interest impelling toward this order. Understand this.

As for what some mentioned—that He, the Exalted, has detailed knowledge of things before their origination, and knowledge is subordinate to the object of knowledge, so things have a certain subsistence in themselves before origination to which knowledge attaches according to that subsistence, and they have resulting interests and eternal predispositions for existence, and good and evil, which He, the Exalted, knows according to that subsistence, then He pours existence upon them here according to what He knew—this is not sound.

Firstly: Because it is built upon His detailed knowledge, the Exalted, of things before origination being acquired knowledge (حصوليا), and its falsehood has been explained in its proper place; rather, it is presential knowledge (حضوري) and is not subordinate to the object of knowledge, but rather the matter is the reverse.

Secondly: Because of the unintelligibility of subsistence (الثبوت) before existence (الوجود), since existence is synonymous with thingness (الشيئية); so that which has no existence has no thingness, and that which has no thingness has no subsistence.

Thirdly: Because the establishment of predisposition (الاستعداد) there is not completed except with the assumption of an actuality (فعلية) corresponding to it, and similarly the assumption of interest is not completed except with the assumption of perfection and deficiency. These are external effects exclusive to external existences; thus, what was assumed as subsistence before origination returns to being an external existence after it, which is a contradiction. This is what the intellectual investigation yields and the Quranic investigation supports. Sufficient for this is His saying, the Exalted: ويوم يقول كن فيكون قوله الحق — and the day He says, ‘Be!’ it is. His word is the truth (6:73). He considered the word "Be!" (كن)—which is that by which things are originated, i.e., their existence attributed to Him—to be a "word" (قولا) of His own, and mentioned that it is the "Truth" (الحق), i.e., the external fixed reality. So His word is the external existence of things, and it is His act as well. Thus, His word is His act, and His word and act are the existence of things externally. He said: الحق من ربك فلا تكن من الممترين — The truth is from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters. (3:60). Truth (الحق) is the word or belief from the aspect that the external world corresponds to it; so the external world is truth primarily, and the word or belief is truth following its correspondence. If the external world is His act, the Exalted, and the external world is the principle of the word and belief, then the truth begins from Him, the Exalted, and returns to Him. Therefore, He said: الحق من ربك (The truth is from your Lord) and did not say: "The truth is with your Lord," as we say in disputes among us: "The truth is with so-and-so."

From here it appears that every act is subject to questioning except His act, the Glorified. For what is sought by the question is the demonstration of the act's being correspondent (مطابقا)—in the passive voice—to the truth. This only applies to that which is not the truth itself. As for the truth itself, it is truth by its essence, without needing correspondence.

His saying, the Exalted: أم اتخذوا من دونه آلهة قل هاتوا برهانكم — Have they taken gods besides Him? Say, ‘Produce your proof!’ (21:24), until the end of the verse. Produce (هاتوا) is a verbal noun meaning "bring it." Proof (البرهان) is the evidence that yields knowledge. What is meant by "reminder" (الذكر)—as derived from the context—is the Book sent down from Allah. Thus, what is meant by "the reminder of those who are with me" is the Quran sent down to him, which is the reminder for his community until the Day of Resurrection; and by "the reminder of those who were before me" the books of the previous prophets, such as the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and others.

It is possible that what is meant by it is the revelation (الوحي) sent down to him in the Quran, which is the reminder of those with him (peace be upon him and his progeny), and the revelation sent down to those before him regarding the matter of the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة) narrated in the Quran. Thus, what is pointed to by this is what is in the Quran of the command for the monotheism of worship sent down to him and sent down to those prophets who preceded him (peace be upon them). Some have interpreted "reminder" as "report" and other things, but they are not to be heeded.

In the verse is the refutation of another possibility among the possibilities that contradict the establishment of the Resurrection and the previously mentioned accounting—namely, that they take gods besides Allah, the Glorified, and worship them, thereby becoming independent of the worship of Allah and His guardianship (ولايته), which necessitates the return to Him, His accounting, and the obligation of answering the call of His prophets. This possibility is refuted by the lack of evidence for it. He has contended with them by commanding the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) to demand proof from them by saying: قل هاتوا برهانكم... etc.

His saying: قل هاتوا برهانكم هذا ذكر من معي وذكر من قبلي is of the type of "denial with evidence"—in the terminology of the art of debate. Its summary is the opponent's demand for proof from the claimant for his unproven claim, basing his demand on the fact that he has evidence indicating the contrary.

He, the Exalted, says to His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny): Say to these who take gods besides Allah: "Produce your proof for your claim, for a claim without evidence is not heard, and it is not intellectually permissible to rely upon it." That which I rely upon in demanding proof is that the heavenly books sent down from Allah, the Glorified, do not agree with you in what you claimed, but rather oppose you in it. So this is the Quran, which is the reminder of those with me, and these are the other books like the Torah, the Gospel, and others, which are the reminder of those before me; they mention the restriction of divinity to Him, the Exalted, alone, and the obligation of His worship.

Or: that which is in the Quran of the revelation sent down to me—which is the reminder of those with me—and the revelation sent down to those prophets who preceded me—which is the reminder of those before me—regarding the matter of worshipping the God, restricts divinity and worship to Him, the Exalted.

His saying: بل أكثرهم لا يعلمون الحق فهم معرضون — But most of them do not know the truth, so they are disregardful. (21:24) is a return to addressing the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), indicating that most of them do not distinguish truth from falsehood; they are not among the people of discernment who follow nothing but evidence. Thus, they are turning away from the truth and its following.

His saying, the Exalted: وما أرسلنا من قبلك من رسول إلا نوحي إليه أنه لا إله إلا أنا فاعبدون — We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me.’ (21:25). This is a confirmation of what was said in the previous verse: that the reminder mentions His monotheism and the obligation of His worship. It is not devoid of some support for the second of the two meanings of "reminder."

His saying: نوحي إليه (We revealed to him) implies continuity. His saying: فاعبدون (so worship Me) is an address to the messengers and those with them from their communities. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وقالوا اتخذ الرحمن ولدا سبحانه بل عباد مكرمون — And they say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather they are [His] honored servants. (21:26). The apparent context testifies that it is a narration of the idolaters' saying that the angels are His children, the Glorified. Thus, what is meant by "honored servants" is the angels. Allah has sanctified Himself from that by His saying: سبحانه (Immaculate is He!), then mentioned the reality of their state through transition (الإضراب).

Since His subsequent saying: لا يسبقونه بالقول (They do not precede Him in speech)... etc., is an explanation of the perfection of their servitude from the aspect of effects, and its purity from the aspect of characteristics and consequences, and He had previously mentioned their being servants, the apparent meaning is that what is meant by their being honored is their being honored through servitude (العبودية), not through anything else. Thus, the meaning boils down to their being servants in the true sense of servitude, and the evidence for it is the proceeding of its perfect effects from them.

What is meant by their being servants (عبادا)—while all possessors of consciousness are servants of Allah—is their being honored in their own selves through servitude, such that they witness nothing of themselves except that they are servants. What is meant by their being honored (مكرمون) is His honoring them, the Exalted, by pouring complete servitude upon them. This is similar to the servant being "sincere" (مخلصا)—with a kasrah on the lam—to his Lord, and His meeting that, the Exalted, by making him "purified" (مخلصا)—with a fathah on the lam—for Himself. The only difference between the honor of the angels and that of humans is that in humans it is acquired (اكتسابي), unlike in the angels. As for His honoring, the Exalted, it is a gift (موهبي) in both groups. Understand this well.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ "They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command" (21:27). To say that one person does not precede another in speech means that he does not say anything until the other has spoken; thus, his speech is subordinate (تبع). Sometimes this is used as a metonymy (يكنى به) for will (الإرادة) and volition (المشية), meaning that his will is subordinate to His will. His saying: وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ "and they act by His command"—the adverbial phrase is connected to "they act" (يعملون) and is placed before it to signify restriction (الحصر); that is, they act only by His command and not by the command of anyone else. The meaning is not merely that they do not act by the command of another, but rather that their action is subordinate to His command—meaning His will—just as their speech is subordinate to His speech. Thus, they are followers of their Lord in both word and deed.

In other words, their will and their action are both subordinate to His will—considering that "speech" is a metonymy for "will"—so they do not will except what He wills, and they do not act except as He wills. This is the perfection of servitude (العبودية), for the necessary implication of a servant's servitude is that his will and his action are owned (مملوكين) by his master.

This is what the apparent meaning of the verse provides, assuming that "command" (الأمر) refers to that which stands in contrast to "prohibition" (النهي). The verse thus implies that the angels do not know prohibition, since prohibition is predicated upon the possibility of performing the prohibited act, whereas they do not act except by command.

It is also possible to derive the reality of the meaning of His command (أمره), the Exalted, from His saying: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it, ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (36:82-83), and His saying: وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye" (54:50). Discussion on this has already preceded in some earlier sections, and a full inquiry will follow in a specific discourse regarding the angels and what the Quran provides concerning the reality of the angel (الملك).

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَما خَلْفَهُمْ وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى وَهُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ "He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not intercede except for someone He approves of, and they are apprehensive for fear of Him" (21:28). The commentators have interpreted "what is before them and what is behind them" (ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَما خَلْفَهُمْ) as the deeds they have already sent forth and those they have yet to do. The meaning would then be: He knows what they have done and what they will do.

Thus, His saying: يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَما خَلْفَهُمْ "He knows what is before them and what is behind them" is a new sentence (استئناف) serving as a justification (التعليل) for what preceded it in His saying: لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ "They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command." It is as if it were said: They do not venture upon any word or deed without His command, the Exalted, only because He knows what they have sent forth of speech and action and what they have deferred; thus, they remain constantly watchful of their states since they are aware of this.

This is a sound meaning in itself, but it is only suitable as a justification for their not venturing into disobedience (المعصية), not as a justification for restricting their actions to the scope of His command, which is what is required. Furthermore, the wording of the verse does not indicate that they know this; and were it not for that, the explanation would be incomplete.

It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying, the Exalted: وَما نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ لَهُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِينا وَما خَلْفَنا وَما بَيْنَ ذلِكَ وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between that, and your Lord is not forgetful" (19:64), that the most appropriate view is to interpret "what is before us" (ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِينا) as the deeds and effects branching out from their existence, and "what is behind us" (ما خَلْفَنا) as the causes of their existence that preceded them and were realized before them. If these two phrases in this verse were interpreted as they were there, the sentence would be a clear justification for the entirety of His saying: بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ "Rather they are [His] honored servants" (21:26) up to His saying: بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ "they act by His command," which mentions the nobility of their essence (الذات) and the nobility of the effects of that essence in speech and action. The meaning would then be: Allah has only honored their essences and praised their effects because He knows their actions and words—which are "what is before them"—and He knows the cause by which they exist and the origin upon which they were established—which is "what is behind them." This is like saying: "So-and-so is noble of soul and praiseworthy of conduct because his deeds are pleasing and he comes from a noble family."

His saying: وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى "and they do not intercede except for someone He approves of" addresses their intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — for others. This is what the idolaters (الوثنيون) clung to in their worship of the angels, as indicated by their saying: هؤُلاءِ شُفَعاؤُنا عِنْدَ اللهِ "These are our intercessors with Allah" (10:18) and ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللهِ زُلْفى "We do not worship them except that they may bring us near to Allah in proximity" (39:3). Thus, the Exalted refuted them by stating that the angels only intercede for those whom Allah approves of. What is meant is the approval of their religion (دين), due to His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48). Thus, faith in Allah without polytheism (الشرك) is the "approval" (الارتضاء). The idolaters are polytheists; and it is a strange matter of theirs that they associate partners with the very angels who do not intercede except for those who are not polytheists, namely the monotheists (الموحدون).

His saying: وَهُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ "and they are apprehensive for fear of Him" refers to the fear (الخشية) of His displeasure and punishment, despite being secure from it due to their lack of disobedience. This is because Allah's placing them in security from punishment through the infallibility (العصمة) He has bestowed upon them does not limit His power (قدرته), the Exalted, nor does it wrest the kingdom from His hand. He possesses, after granting security, the very same power He possessed before it, and He is powerful over all things. By this, the meaning of the following verse becomes upright.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَقُلْ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّي إِلهٌ مِنْ دُونِهِ فَذلِكَ نَجْزِيهِ جَهَنَّمَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ "Should any of them say, ‘I am a god besides Him,’ We will requite him with hell. Thus do We requite the wrongdoers" (21:29). That is, whoever says such a thing would be a wrongdoer (ظالما), and We requite him with Hell because it is the recompense of the wrongdoer. The verse is a conditional proposition (قضية شرطية), and a conditional does not necessitate the actual occurrence of the condition.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما وَجَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ أَفَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ "Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass and We clove them asunder, and We made every living thing out of water? Will they not then have faith?" (21:30). What is meant by "seeing" (الرؤية) is intellectual knowledge (العلم الفكري); it is expressed as "seeing" because of its clarity, as it is the result of reflection upon a sensible matter.

"Closed mass" (الرتق) and "claving asunder" (الفتق) are two opposite meanings. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "الرتق (the closed mass) is joining and coalescing, whether by nature or by craft; the Exalted said: كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما. And he said: الفتق (the claving asunder) is the separation between two connected things, and it is the opposite of ratq." [End of quote]. The dual pronoun in كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما refers to the heavens and the earth, as the heavens are one group and the earth is another group, so they are two groups. The predicate, namely ratqan (رتقا), appears in the singular because it is a verbal noun (مصدر), even if it carries the meaning of a passive participle (المفعول). The meaning is: these two groups were joined and connected, and then We separated them.

This verse and the three verses following it are a proof (برهان) for His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — in His Lordship (الربوبية) over the entire world. He brought them forth in connection with the discourse leading to His oneness and the negation of those they took as gods besides Allah, considering the angels—who are among the gods according to them—as His children. They based this on the idea that creation and origination (الإيجاد) belong to Allah, while Lordship and governance (التدبير) belong to the gods. Thus, the Glorified presented in these verses things from creation whose creation is intertwined with the governance of their affairs. It thereby became clear that governance is inseparable from creation; thus, it is necessary that the One who created them is the One who governs their affairs. This applies to the heavens, the earth, every living thing, the mountains, the mountain passes, the night, the day, the sun, and the moon, in their creation and the states mentioned by the Glorified.

So His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما "Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass and We clove them asunder"—the "faithless" (الذين كفروا), according to the context, are the idolaters, as they distinguish between creation and governance by attributing creation to Allah, the Glorified, and governance to gods besides Him. He has demonstrated their error in this distinction by turning their attention to that which cannot be doubted: the claving asunder of the heavens and the earth after they were a solid mass. For in that is a creation inseparable from governance; how then can their creation be established by one and their governance by others?

We constantly observe the separation of terrestrial and atmospheric compounds from one another, the separation of plant species from the earth, animals from animals, and humans from humans. The separated entity appears through separation in a new form with new effects and properties, after having been connected to its origin from which it separated, without distinct existence, apparent effect, or manifest rule. These actualities (الفعليات) were preserved in existence within the potentiality (القوة), deposited as essences in the matter (المادة), as a solid mass without claving, until they were cloven after the mass and appeared with the actuality of their essences and effects.

The heavens and the earth, with their celestial bodies, are in the same state as the individuals of the species we have mentioned. Although our short lifespans do not allow us to witness in these celestial bodies and the earth we inhabit what we witness in the particular entities (الكينونات الجزئية) mentioned—seeing the beginning of their existence or the collapse of their being—nevertheless, matter is matter, its rules are its rules, and the laws (القوانين) governing it do not differ or fail.

The repeated separation of the parts of compounds and offspring from the earth, and the like in the atmosphere, points us to a day when all were a solid mass, joined and not separated from the earth. Likewise, it guides us to a stage where there was no distinction between heaven and earth, and all were a solid mass; then Allah clove them under an organized and precise governance, through which each appeared with its own actuality of essence and effects.

This is what a simple observation of the existence (كينونة) of this witnessed world provides, in its celestial and terrestrial parts—an existence intertwined with governance and concurrent with the system (النظام) prevailing in all. Modern scientific research has brought this view closer, as it has clarified that the bodies under sensory perception are composed of a limited number of common elements, and each has a limited duration and a fixed lifespan, though they differ in length and brevity.

This is if what is meant by the "solid mass" (رتق) of the heavens and the earth is the lack of distinction of some from others, and by "claving asunder" (فتق) the distinction of the heavens from the earth. But if what is meant by their "solid mass" is the lack of separation between the parts of each in itself—such that nothing descends from the sky and nothing comes out of the earth—and by their "claving asunder" the opposite of that, then the meaning would be that the heavens were a solid mass that did not rain, so We clove them with rain, and the earth was a solid mass that did not sprout, so We clove it with vegetation. The proof would then be complete, and it might be supported by His subsequent saying: وَجَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ "and We made every living thing out of water." However, this would be specific among all events to raining and sprouting, unlike the proof according to the first explanation.

Some commentators mentioned, and others approved, that what is meant by the "solid mass" of the heavens and the earth is the lack of distinction of some from others in their prior state of non-existence (العدم), and by their "claving asunder" the distinction of some from others in existence (الوجود) after non-existence. Thus, it would be an argument based on the temporal production (حدوث) of the heavens and the earth for the existence of their Producer (محدثها), who is Allah, the Glorified.

The problem with this is that while the argument from temporal production to a Producer is sound in itself, it is of no use against the idolaters who acknowledge His existence, the Exalted, and the attribution of origination to Him—and the discourse is directed at them. Rather, what is useful against them is the argument by which the attribution of governance (التدبير) to Him, the Exalted, is established, in contrast to their attributing governance to their gods and basing worship upon that.

His saying: وَجَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ "and We made every living thing out of water"—the apparent context is that "making" (الجعل) means creating (الخلق), and "every living thing" is its object. The meaning is that water has a complete role in the existence of living beings, as He said: وَاللهُ خَلَقَ كُلَّ دَابَّةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ "Allah created every moving creature from water" (24:45). Perhaps the occurrence of this statement in the context of enumerating sensible signs (الآيات المحسوسة) necessitates the exclusion of the angels and those like them from this rule. The connection of life to water has become clear through modern scientific research.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ رَواسِيَ أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ وَجَعَلْنا فِيها فِجاجاً سُبُلاً لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ "And We set firm mountains in the earth lest it should sway with them, and We set in it wide courses for ways, so that they may be guided" (21:31). It is said in al-Majma': "الرواسي (the firm mountains) are the mountains; something rasa (رست) when it becomes fixed by its weight, so it is rasiyah (راسة), just as a ship tarsu (ترسو) when it stops and becomes steady in its position. الميد (swaying) is agitation by moving in different directions. الفج (the wide course) is a wide path between two mountains." [End of quote].

The meaning is: We placed in the earth fixed mountains so that the earth would not tilt and agitate with them, and We placed in those mountains wide paths which are ways, so that they might be guided through them to their destinations and homelands. In this is an indication that mountains have a specific connection to earthquakes, and were it not for them, the earth would agitate with its crust.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنَا السَّماءَ سَقْفاً مَحْفُوظاً وَهُمْ عَنْ آياتِها مُعْرِضُونَ "And We made the sky a protected roof, yet they are disregardful of its signs" (21:32). It seems that what is meant by the sky being "protected" (محفوظا) is its protection from the devils, as He said: وَحَفِظْناها مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ "and We have guarded it from every outcast devil" (15:17). What is meant by the "signs of the sky" (آيات السماء) are the various celestial events that indicate the unity of governance and its attribution to their one Originator.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ "It is He who created the night and the day, the sun and the moon, each swimming in an orbit" (21:33). The verse is apparent in establishing an orbit (الفلك) for each of the following: the night—which is the conical shadow attached to the face of the earth opposite to the sun; the day—which is the opposite of the night; and the sun and the moon. Thus, what is meant by "orbit" (الفلك) is the path of each of them.

Furthermore, what is intended is the explanation of the positions and states occurring relative to the earth and in its atmosphere, even if the state of other celestial bodies is different. For there is no night or day corresponding to it for the sun and other fixed stars, which are luminous by essence; and for the moon and other planets that acquire light, their night and day are different from ours.

His saying: يَسْبَحُونَ "swimming" is from al-sabh (السبح), meaning moving through water by cleaving it. It is said that He used the form يَسْبَحُونَ (the plural for rational beings) because He attributed to them the action of rational beings, as He said: وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِي ساجِدِينَ "and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrating to me" (12:4).

(Commentary)

In al-Mahasin, with its chain of transmission from Yunus in a marfu’ report, he said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: ليس من باطل يقوم بإزاء حق ـ إلا غلب الحق الباطل وذلك قول الله : « بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَى الْباطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُ فَإِذا هُوَ زاهِقٌ » — "There is no falsehood that stands against a truth except that the truth overcomes the falsehood, and that is the saying of Allah: 'Rather We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and behold, it vanishes.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Ayyub ibn al-Hurr, he said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: يا أيوب ما من أحد إلا وقد يرد عليه الحق ـ حتى يصدع قلبه قبله أم تركه ـ وذلك أن الله يقول في كتابه : « بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَى الْباطِلِ ـ فَيَدْمَغُهُ فَإِذا هُوَ زاهِقٌ وَلَكُمُ الْوَيْلُ مِمَّا تَصِفُونَ » — "O Ayyub, there is no one but that the truth is presented to him until it cleaves his heart—whether he accepts it or leaves it—and that is because Allah says in His Book: 'Rather We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and behold, it vanishes. And woe to you for what you allege.'"

I say: The two narrations are based on the generalization of the verse.

In al-Uyun, in the chapter on what has been narrated from al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding Harut and Marut, in a hadith: "That the angels are infallible (معصومون)—protected from disbelief and foul deeds by the graces of Allah, the Exalted." Allah, the Exalted, said regarding them: لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ — "they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they carry out what they are commanded" (66:6). And the Mighty and Majestic said: وَلَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ — meaning the angels — لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَلا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ — "To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth, and those who are with Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they weary. They celebrate His glory night and day, and they do not flag" (21:19-20).

In Nahj al-Balaghah, he (peace be upon him) said in describing the angels: ومسبحون لا يسأمون ، ولا يغشاهم نوم العيون ، ولا سهو العقول ، ولا فترة الأبدان ، ولا غفلة النسيان — "And they are glorifiers who do not tire; they are not overcome by the sleep of eyes, nor the oversight of intellects, nor the flagging of bodies, nor the heedlessness of forgetfulness."

I say: By this, what is found in some narrations that the angels sleep is weakened, as in the book Kamal al-Din, with its chain of transmission from Dawud ibn Farqad from some of our companions from Abu ‘Abdillah: that he was asked about the angels, do they sleep? He said: ما من حي إلا وهو ينام خلا الله وحده — "There is no living thing but that it sleeps, except Allah alone." I said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: 'They celebrate His glory night and day, and they do not flag'?" He said: أنفاسهم تسبيح — "Their breaths are glorification." Furthermore, the narration is weak.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, he said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "What is the proof that Allah is One?" He said: اتصال التدبير وتمام الصنع كما قال عز وجل : « لَوْ كانَ فِيهِما آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللهُ لَفَسَدَتا » — "The continuity of governance and the perfection of craftsmanship, as the Mighty and Majestic said: 'Had there been any gods in them besides Allah, they would surely have fallen apart.'"

I say: This supports what we previously mentioned in the presentation of the proof.

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from ‘Amr ibn Jabir, he said: I said to Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "O son of the Messenger of Allah, we see children, some of whom are born dead, some are miscarried incomplete, some are born blind, mute, or deaf, some die the moment they fall to the ground, some remain until puberty, and some live until they become old men. How is that, and what is the reason for it?"

He (peace be upon him) said: إن الله تبارك وتعالى ـ أولى بما يدبره من أمر خلقه منهم ـ وهو الخالق والمالك لهم فمن منعه التعمير ـ فإنما منعه ما ليس له ، ومن عمره فإنما أعطاه ما ليس له ـ فهو المتفضل بما أعطى ـ وعادل فيما منع ولا يسأل عما يفعل وهم يسألون — "Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, is more entitled to govern the affairs of His creation than they are. He is the Creator and the Owner of them. So whoever He denies long life, He has only denied him what was not his; and whoever He grants long life, He has only given him what was not his. Thus, He is the Bestower of grace in what He gives, and Just in what He withholds; 'He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned.'"

Jabir said: I said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, and how is He not questioned about what He does?" He said: لأنه لا يفعل إلا ما كان حكمة وصوابا ، وهو المتكبر الجبار والواحد القهار ، فمن وجد في نفسه حرجا في شيء مما قضى كفر ـ ومن أنكر شيئا من أفعاله جحد — "Because He does not do anything except that it is wisdom and correctness. He is the All-Magnanimous, the All-Compeller, the One, the All-Subduer. So whoever finds in himself any grievance regarding anything He has decreed has disbelieved, and whoever denies any of His acts has rejected [the truth]."

I say: This is a noble narration that provides a universal principle (أصلا كليا) regarding good and evil: that good things are existential matters (أمور وجودية) that trace back to His giving and grace, the Exalted, while evil things are privative matters (أمور عدمية) that end in the non-giving of what the servant does not own. As for his (peace be upon him) saying that He, the Exalted, is more entitled to what belongs to His servant than the servant himself, its reason is that He, the Exalted, is the Owner of the servant's essence, and the servant only owns what he owns through the ownership granted by Him, the Exalted; thus He is the Owner of what He has made the servant own, and the servant's ownership is longitudinal (في طول) to His ownership.

His saying: "Because He does not do anything except that it is wisdom and correctness" is an allusion to the first presentation we provided. His saying: "He is the All-Magnanimous, the All-Compeller, the One, the All-Subduer" is an allusion to the second presentation we mentioned in the commentary of the verse.

In Nur al-Thaqalayn, from al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, said: يا بن آدم بمشيتي كنت أنت الذي تشاء لنفسك ما تشاء ، وبقوتي أديت إلي فرائضي ، وبنعمتي قويت على معصيتي ـ جعلتك سميعا بصيرا قويا ـ ما أصابك من حسنة فمن الله ـ وما أصابك من سيئة فمن نفسك ـ وذلك أني أولى بحسناتك منك ـ وأنت أولى بسيئاتك مني ـ وذلك أني لا أسأل عما أفعل وهم يسألون — "O son of Adam, by My will you were the one who wills for yourself what you will; by My power you performed My obligations to Me; and by My blessing you were strong enough to disobey Me. I made you hearing, seeing, and strong. Whatever good befalls you is from Allah, and whatever evil befalls you is from yourself. That is because I am more entitled to your good deeds than you are, and you are more entitled to your evil deeds than I am. That is because I am not questioned about what I do, but they are questioned."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: هذا ذِكْرُ مَنْ مَعِيَ وَذِكْرُ مَنْ قَبْلِي — "This is a reminder of those who are with me and a reminder of those before me" (21:24)—Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: يعني بذكر من معي ما هو كائن ـ وبذكر من قبلي ما قد كان — "He means by the reminder of those with me what is to be, and by the reminder of those before me what has been."

In al-Uyun, with its chain of transmission to al-Husayn ibn Khalid from ‘Ali ibn Musa al-Rida from his father from his forefathers from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: من لم يؤمن بحوضي فلا أورده الله حوضي ، ومن لم يؤمن بشفاعتي فلا أناله الله شفاعتي ، ثم قال عليه‌السلام : إنما شفاعتي لأهل الكبائر من أمتي ـ فأما المحسنون فما عليهم من سبيل — "Whoever does not believe in my Pond (الحوض), may Allah not bring him to my Pond; and whoever does not believe in my intercession, may Allah not grant him my intercession." Then he (peace be upon him) said: "My intercession is only for the people of major sins from my community; as for the virtuous, there is no way against them."

Al-Husayn ibn Khalid said: I said to al-Rida (peace be upon him): "O son of the Messenger of Allah, then what is the meaning of the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'and they do not intercede except for someone He approves of'?" He said: لا يشفعون إلا لمن ارتضى الله دينه — "They do not intercede except for someone whose religion Allah approves of."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated it, as did al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th, from Jabir: that the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) recited the saying of Allah: وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى and then said: إن شفاعتي لأهل الكبائر من أمتي — "Indeed, my intercession is for the people of major sins from my community."

In al-Ihtijaj, it is narrated: that ‘Amr ibn ‘Ubayd came to Muhammad ibn ‘Ali al-Baqir (peace be upon him) to test him with questions. He said to him: "May I be your ransom, what is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: 'Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass and We clove them asunder'—what is this solid mass (الرتق) and claving asunder (الفتق)?" Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: كانت السماء رتقا لا تنزل القطر ـ وكانت الأرض رتقا لا تخرج النبات ـ ففتق الله السماء بالقطر وفتق الأرض بالنبات — "The sky was a solid mass that did not send down rain, and the earth was a solid mass that did not bring forth vegetation; so Allah clove the sky with rain and clove the earth with vegetation." ‘Amr ibn ‘Ubayd was silenced, found no objection, and left.

I say: This meaning is narrated in Rawdat al-Kafi from him (peace be upon him) through two chains.

In Nahj al-Balaghah, he (peace be upon him) said: وفتق بعد الارتتاق صوامت أبوابها — "And He clove, after the state of being a solid mass, the silent ones of its doors."

Ayahs

وَمَا جَعَلْنَا لِبَشَرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ ٱلْخُلْدَ ۖ أَفَإِي۟ن مِّتَّ فَهُمُ ٱلْخَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٤)
We did not give immortality to any human before you. If you are fated to die, will they live on forever? (34)
كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۗ وَنَبْلُوكُم بِٱلشَّرِّ وَٱلْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً ۖ وَإِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ (٣٥)
Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of test, and to Us you will be brought back. (35)
وَإِذَا رَءَاكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى يَذْكُرُ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ وَهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٦)
Whenever the faithless see you they only take you in derision: ‘Is this the one who remembers your gods?’ while they remain defiant towards the remembrance of the All-beneficent. (36)
خُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ ۚ سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ (٣٧)
Man is a creature of haste. Soon I will show you My signs. So do not ask Me to hasten. (37)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٨)
And they say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (38)
لَوْ يَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ حِينَ لَا يَكُفُّونَ عَن وُجُوهِهِمُ ٱلنَّارَ وَلَا عَن ظُهُورِهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٣٩)
If only the faithless knew about the time when they will not be able to keep the Fire off their faces and their backs, nor will they be helped! (39)
بَلْ تَأْتِيهِم بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ رَدَّهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٤٠)
Indeed, it will overtake them suddenly, dumbfounding them. So they will neither be able to avert it, nor will they be granted any respite. (40)
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٤١)
Apostles were certainly derided before you; but those who ridiculed them were besieged by what they had been deriding. (41)
قُلْ مَن يَكْلَؤُكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۗ بَلْ هُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٤٢)
Say, ‘Who can guard you, day and night, against [the punishment of] the All-beneficent [should He want to punish you]?’ Indeed, they are disregardful of their Lord’s remembrance. (42)
أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ تَمْنَعُهُم مِّن دُونِنَا ۚ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَا هُم مِّنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ (٤٣)
Do they have gods besides Us to defend them? Neither can they help themselves, nor can they shield [the idolaters] from Us. (43)
بَلْ مَتَّعْنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ طَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْعُمُرُ ۗ أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِى ٱلْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَآ ۚ أَفَهُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٤٤)
Indeed, We have provided for them and their fathers until they lived on for long years. Do they not see how We visit the land diminishing it at its edges? Are they the ones who will prevail? (44)
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُنذِرُكُم بِٱلْوَحْىِ ۚ وَلَا يَسْمَعُ ٱلصُّمُّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا مَا يُنذَرُونَ (٤٥)
Say, ‘I indeed warn you by the means of revelation.’ But the deaf do not hear the call when they are warned. (45)
وَلَئِن مَّسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٤٦)
Should a whiff of your Lord’s punishment touch them, they will surely say, ‘Woe to us! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (46)
وَنَضَعُ ٱلْمَوَٰزِينَ ٱلْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَلَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنَا بِهَا ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِنَا حَـٰسِبِينَ (٤٧)
We shall set up just scales on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least. Even if it be the weight of a mustard seed We shall produce it and We suffice as reckoners. (47)

(Commentary)

As a continuation of the discourse regarding prophethood (النبوة), this section mentions some of what the polytheists said concerning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), such as their saying: "He will die, and then we shall be rid of him and find rest," and their saying in mockery of him: أَهذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ — "Is this the one who mentions your gods?" (21:36). It also mentions their saying in mockery of the Resurrection (البعث) and the Day of Judgment (القيامة) with which they were warned: مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?" (21:38). Included herein is the response to their claims, a warning and threat directed at them, and consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ أَفَإِنْ مِتَّ فَهُمُ الْخالِدُونَ — We did not grant immortality to any human before you. If you die, are they then to be immortal? (21:34). It appears from the verse that they used to console themselves with the idea that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would eventually die, thereby freeing them from his call and saving their gods from his criticism, as has been narrated about them in verses such as: نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ — "We await for him some calamity of fate" (52:30). Thus, He responded to this by stating that We have not granted immortality (الخلد) to any human being before you such that this could be expected for you; rather, you shall die and they too shall die. Your death will not benefit them in the least, for they will not attain immortality by your death. Everyone is mortal. Furthermore, their short, term-limited lives are not free from trial (الفتنة) and divine testing (الامتحان الإلهي), for no human is exempt from this in the life of this world. Nor are they beyond Our dominion in the Hereafter; rather, to Us they shall return, and We shall hold them to account and recompense them for what they have done.

His saying: أَفَإِنْ مِتَّ فَهُمُ الْخالِدُونَ — "If you die, are they then to be immortal?" He did not simply say "they are immortal." The interrogative here is for denial (الإنكار), serving to negate the restriction of the heart (قصر القلب). It is as if it were said: Their saying, "We await for him some calamity of fate," is the speech of one who sees for himself an immortality in which you are a competitor; as if were you to die, he would walk away with immortality, seize it, and live a perpetual, pleasant, and luxurious life. But it is not so; rather, every soul shall taste death, and the life of this world is built upon trial and testing. There is no meaning to a permanent trial or an eternal test; rather, they must return to their Lord so He may recompense them for that through which He tested and distinguished them.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً وَإِلَيْنا تُرْجَعُونَ — Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with evil and good by way of trial, and to Us you will be brought back. (21:35). The term soul (النفس) — based on what reflection upon its instances of usage yields — has a primary meaning that is identical to the meaning of that to which it is annexed (المضاف إليه). Thus, the "soul of a thing" means the thing itself; the "soul of a human" means the human himself; and the "soul of a stone" means the stone itself. If it were severed from annexation, it would have no realized meaning. Based on this meaning, it is used for verbal emphasis (التأكيد اللفظي), as when we say: "Zayd himself (نفسه) came to me," or to convey its essence, as when we say: "The self (نفس) of Zayd came to me."

In this sense, it is applied to everything, even to Him, the Exalted, as He said: كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ — "He has made mercy incumbent upon Himself" (6:12), and He said: وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ اللهُ نَفْسَهُ — "Allah warns you of Himself" (3:28), and He said: تَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِي وَلا أَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِكَ — "You know what is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self" (5:116).

Subsequently, the usage of this term became common specifically for the human person (شخص الإنسان), which is the entity composed of spirit (روح) and body (بدن). Thus, it acquired a meaning in itself even if severed from annexation. The Exalted said: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْها زَوْجَها — "It is He who created you from a single soul, and made from it its mate" (7:189), meaning from a single human person. And He said: مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسادٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّما قَتَلَ النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً وَمَنْ أَحْياها فَكَأَنَّما أَحْيَا النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً — "whoever slays a soul, without [its being guilty of] slaying a soul or [of] corruption on the earth, it is as though he had slain all mankind; and whoever saves a life, it is as though he had saved all mankind" (5:32), meaning whoever kills a human and whoever gives life to a human. Both meanings are combined in His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجادِلُ عَنْ نَفْسِها — "every soul will come pleading for itself" (16:111); the first "soul" carries the second meaning (the person), and the second "soul" carries the first meaning (the self).

Then, they used it to refer to the human spirit (الروح الإنساني) because life, knowledge, and power — which constitute the human being — subsist through it. From this is the saying of the Exalted: أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ — "Give up your souls! Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment" (6:93).

These two applications — namely the second and third — were not consistently applied to things other than humans, such as plants and other animals, except according to technical scientific terminology. In common usage (عرفا), a single plant or animal is not called a "soul" (نفس), nor is the governing principle of its body called a "soul." Yes, blood is sometimes called "soul" (نفس) because life depends upon it, and from this comes the term "flowing blood" (النفس السائلة).

Similarly, in the language, the term "soul" is not applied in the second or third sense to angels or jinn, even if it is believed that they possess life. The usage of "soul" for them does not appear in the Qur'an either, even though the verses state that the jinn have obligations (تكليف) like humans, as well as death and resurrection (حشر). He said: وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ — "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me" (51:56), and He said: فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ — "among the nations of jinn and humans who passed away before them" (46:18), and He said: وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ قَدِ اسْتَكْثَرْتُمْ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ — "On the day He will muster them all together: ‘O company of jinn, you have certainly [seduced] many of the humans’" (6:128). This is what is gathered regarding the meaning of "soul" according to linguistic custom.

As for death (الموت), it is the loss of life and its effects, such as consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة), from that which is naturally disposed to be characterized by them. The Exalted said: وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ — "you were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will cause you to die" (2:28), and He said regarding idols: أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ — "They are dead, not living" (16:21). As for it being the separation of the soul from the body by the severance of its governing attachment (تعلقها التدبيري), as defined in rational inquiries, or it being the transition from one abode to another, as in the Prophetic tradition, these are meanings revealed by reason (العقل) or tradition (النقل) beyond what is established in common usage. It is well known that death, in the sense mentioned, only characterizes the human being composed of spirit and body with respect to his body; for it is the body that is characterized by the loss of life after possessing it. As for the spirit, nothing has appeared in the speech of the Exalted that speaks of it being characterized by death, just as this has not appeared regarding the angels. As for His saying: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ — "Everything is to perish except His Face" (28:88), and His saying: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "The Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon" (39:68), it will be explained, God willing, that perishing (الهلاك) and swooning (الصعق) are distinct from death, even if they sometimes coincide with it.

Thus, it has become clear from what we have presented: First, that the intended meaning of "soul" in His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ — "Every soul shall taste death" is the human being — which is the second of its three usages — rather than the human spirit, since the attribution of death to the spirit is not established in the speech of the Exalted such that it could be interpreted as such.

Second: That the verse encompasses the human being specifically and not others like angels, jinn, and other animals, even if some of them are characterized by death, such as jinn and animals. A contextual clue (القرينة) for the verse's exclusivity to humans is His preceding saying: وَما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ — "We did not grant immortality to any human before you" and His subsequent saying: وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً — "and We will test you with evil and good by way of trial", as we shall clarify.

A group of commentators mentioned that the intended meaning of "soul" in the verse is the spirit, but you have seen its contrary. Many of them insisted on the verse's generality for every living thing, including humans, angels, jinn, and other animals, and even plants if they possess true life; you have seen the flaws in this.

Among the most peculiar things said in establishing the verse's generality is what Imam al-Razi mentioned in al-Tafsir al-Kabir. After establishing that the verse is general for every possessor of a soul, he stated that the verse is qualified (مخصصة), for the Exalted has a "Self" (نفس) as He said, narrating from Jesus (peace be upon him): تَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِي وَلا أَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِكَ — "You know what is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self", despite death being impossible for Him, the Glorified. Likewise, inanimate objects have "souls" (نفوس) yet they do not die. He then said: "A qualified general term (العام المخصوص) remains a proof, so it continues to be applied according to its literal meaning (ظاهره) regarding everything except what was excluded. This invalidates the view of the philosophers regarding human spirits, detached intellects (العقول المفارقة), and celestial souls (النفوس الفلكية) that they do not die." End quote.

In this, there are several points: First, the "soul" in the sense applied to the Exalted and to everything is the "soul" in the first of the three usages we presented, which is only used when annexed, as in the verse he cited as evidence. However, the "soul" in the verse under discussion is severed from annexation, so it is certainly not intended in that sense. Thus, "soul" remains in one of the other two meanings, and you have seen that the third meaning is also not intended, so the second remains.

Second: His denial of death for inanimate objects contradicts the saying of the Exalted: كُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ — "you were lifeless and He gave you life" and His saying: أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ — "They are dead, not living", and other such verses.

Third: His claim that the verse's generality invalidates the philosophers' view regarding human spirits, detached intellects, and celestial souls is an error. These are rational issues (مسائل عقلية) sought through the path of demonstration (البرهان). A demonstration is a proof that yields certainty (اليقين). If the arguments they established for them are all or some of them demonstrations, as they claim, then no literal appearance (الظهور) from the verse can be established against them, for literal appearance is a probabilistic proof (حجة ظنية). How can the coexistence of knowledge (العلم) with a probabilistic opinion (الظن) to the contrary be conceived? And if they are not demonstrations, then the issues are not proven, and there is no need for a probabilistic opinion to the contrary.

Furthermore, His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ — "Every soul shall taste death", just as it is a confirmation and establishment of the content of His preceding saying: وَما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ — "We did not grant immortality to any human before you" etc., it is also a preamble and preparation for His subsequent saying: وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً — "and We will test you with evil and good by way of trial" — meaning: We test you with what you dislike, such as illness and poverty, and what you desire, such as health and wealth, as a trial. It is as if it were said: We give each of you a limited, term-bound life and We test you therein with evil and good as a trial, then to your Lord you shall return, and He will judge for you and against you.

In this is an indication of the reason for the necessity of death for every living soul: that the life of every soul is a life of trial and tribulation (ابتلاء). It is well known that a trial is a preliminary matter (أمر مقدمي), and it is necessary that a preliminary does not last forever without reaching an end. It is also necessary that behind every preliminary there is an objective (ذو مقدمة), and after every trial there is a station where its result is determined. Thus, for every living soul, there is an inevitable death, followed by a return to Allah, the Glorified, for the final judgment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَآكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً أَهذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ وَهُمْ بِذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ — When the faithless see you, they only take you in derision: ‘Is this the one who mentions your gods?’ while they are faithless at the mention of the All-beneficent. (21:36). The particle إِنْ is negatory, and the meaning of His saying: إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً is the restriction (قصر) of their treatment of him to taking him as a mockery; that is, they do not treat you except as a mockery to be ridiculed.

His saying: أَهذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ — "Is this the one who mentions your gods?" — the implied meaning being "they say" or "saying: Is this the one..." — is a narration of their words of mockery. The mockery lies in referring to him with this description. Their intention was his mentioning their gods with evil, but they did not state it explicitly out of "etiquette" toward their gods. This is similar to His saying: قالُوا سَمِعْنا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقالُ لَهُ إِبْراهِيمُ — "They said, ‘We heard a youth mentioning them, who is called Abraham’" (21:60).

His saying: وَهُمْ بِذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ — "while they are faithless at the mention of the All-beneficent" is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال) from the pronoun in "they take you," or from the subject of the implied "they say," which is closer. The gist is that they are indignant on behalf of their gods against you when you say of them that they neither benefit nor harm — which is a word of truth — so they confront you only with mockery and insult; yet they are not indignant on behalf of Allah when His mention is disbelieved, and they themselves are the disbelievers.

The intended meaning of "the mention of the All-beneficent" (ذكر الرحمن) is mentioning Him, the Exalted, as the Bestower of every mercy and the Giver of every blessing, the necessary consequence of which is that He, the Exalted, is the Lord Whom it is obligatory to worship. It has also been said that the intended meaning of "the mention" is the Qur'an.

The meaning is: When those who disbelieve — namely the polytheists — see you, they do not take you nor deal with you except with mockery and ridicule, saying to one another: "Is this the one who mentions your gods?" — meaning with evil. Thus, they are indignant for their gods when they are mentioned, while they are disbelievers in the mention of the All-beneficent, not considering it a crime nor being indignant for it.

His saying, the Exalted: خُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ سَأُرِيكُمْ آياتِي فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ — Man is a creature of haste. I will show you My signs. So do not ask Me to hasten. (21:37). The polytheists, in addition to their disbelief in the prophetic call, used to mock the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) whenever they saw him, which was an increase in disbelief and rebellion. Mockery of something only occurs by assuming it is a jest (هزل) and not serious (جد), thus meeting jest with jest. However, He, the Exalted, took this mockery of theirs as something serious and not a jest. Thus, mockery after disbelief became an exposure to divine punishment after a prior exposure, which is the seeking of haste (استعجال) for the punishment. For they were not content with the signs that came to them while they were in a state of well-being, and they sought signs that would requite them for what they had done. Therefore, He, the Glorified, considered their mockery after disbelief as seeking haste in seeing the signs — namely the signs accompanied by punishment — and informed them that He would show them these signs.

Thus, His saying: خُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ — "Man is a creature of haste" is a metaphor (كناية) for man's extreme haste, as if he were created from haste itself and knows nothing else. This is similar to saying: "So-and-so is all good" or "all evil," or "created from good" or "from evil." This is more eloquent than saying, "How hasty he is" or "How intense is his seeking of haste."
